title: Prime Number Theorem
body: The Prime Number Theorem describes the asymptotic distribution of prime numbers. Conjectured by Gauss and Legendre, proved by Hadamard and de la Vallee-Poussin in 1896 using complex analysis. The theorem states that pi(N) ~ N/log(N), where pi(N) is the count of primes up to N. The Riemann Hypothesis would give a tighter bound on the error term. This theorem is fundamental to analytic number theory, with deep connections to the Riemann zeta function.

title: Fermat's Last Theorem
body: Fermat's Last Theorem states no three positive integers a,b,c satisfy a^n+b^n=c^n for n>2. Fermat claimed a proof in 1637. Wiles proved it in 1994 using modular forms and elliptic curves, based on the Taniyama-Shimura conjecture. The proof spans over 100 pages and connects number theory to geometry. Andrew Wiles's breakthrough revolutionized arithmetic geometry and opened new connections between modular forms and elliptic curves. The proof was initially announced in 1993, found to contain a gap, and corrected with Richard Taylor's assistance in 1994.

title: Riemann Hypothesis
body: The Riemann Hypothesis, one of seven Millennium Prize Problems, asserts all non-trivial zeros of the Riemann zeta function have real part 1/2. Riemann introduced it in 1859. It has profound implications for prime distribution. Over 10^13 zeros are numerically verified on the critical line, but a general proof remains elusive. It is the most important open problem in mathematics.

title: Group Theory
body: Group theory studies algebraic structures called groups, capturing symmetry. A group has an associative operation, identity, and inverses. Galois originated it in the 1830s for polynomial solvability. Key concepts: subgroups, normal subgroups, quotient groups, homomorphisms. The classification of finite simple groups was completed in 2004. Lie groups describe fundamental symmetries in particle physics.

title: Linear Algebra
body: Linear algebra concerns vector spaces and linear maps. Core concepts: vectors, matrices, determinants, eigenvalues, eigenvectors. Developed by Cayley, Sylvester, von Neumann. Key results: spectral theorem, SVD, Gram-Schmidt. Indispensable in machine learning, quantum mechanics, computer graphics, data science, and engineering. Vector spaces, linear transformations, and matrices form the backbone of numerical computation. Applications span solving linear systems, least squares fitting, principal component analysis, PageRank, and quantum state representation.

title: Euclidean Geometry
body: Euclidean geometry is the geometry of flat space, formalized by Euclid in Elements (300 BCE). His axiomatic approach established mathematical rigor. Key results: Pythagorean theorem, Thales' theorem. Independence of the parallel postulate led to non-Euclidean geometries. Essential in surveying, architecture, and navigation. Euclid's parallel postulate remained controversial for centuries, and its independence was eventually proved by the discovery of consistent non-Euclidean geometries by Gauss, Lobachevsky, Bolyai, and Riemann.

title: Differential Geometry
body: Differential geometry applies calculus to curves, surfaces, and manifolds. Gauss's Theorema Egregium (1827) showed curvature is intrinsic. Riemann generalized to n-dimensions, enabling Einstein's general relativity. Key concepts: metric tensor, curvature tensors, geodesics. Applications: computer vision, manifold learning, robotics, and physics. The field's modern development is deeply tied to gauge theories in physics, where curvature concepts generalize to fiber bundles describing fundamental forces.

title: Real Analysis
body: Real analysis rigorously studies real numbers, sequences, continuity, differentiation, integration. Developed by Cauchy, Weierstrass, Riemann, Dedekind in the 19th century. Key concepts: epsilon-delta definition, uniform convergence, Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem, Lebesgue integral. Measure theory provides the foundation for probability. The Lebesgue integral extends the Riemann integral to a wider class of functions and provides the rigorous foundation for Fourier analysis and probability theory used throughout modern mathematics.

title: Complex Analysis
body: Complex analysis studies functions of complex variables. Holomorphic functions are infinitely differentiable and power-series representable. Cauchy's integral formula enables contour integration via residues. The Riemann mapping theorem is fundamental. Crucial for number theory, physics (conformal field theory), and electrical engineering. One of the most elegant branches of mathematics, complex analysis has applications in fluid dynamics, electromagnetism, signal processing, and the study of the Riemann zeta function.

title: Topology
body: Topology studies properties preserved under continuous deformation. Originated from Euler's bridges problem and polyhedron formula. Maturing through Poincare around 1900. Areas: general topology (open sets, compactness), algebraic topology (homotopy, homology), differential topology (manifolds). The Poincare conjecture was proved by Perelman in 2003. Topology underlies gauge theories and topological insulators. The field splits into point-set topology (foundational concepts of continuity and compactness) and algebraic topology (using algebraic invariants like homology and homotopy groups to classify spaces).

title: Probability Theory
body: Probability theory quantifies uncertainty. Kolmogorov axiomatized it in 1933. Key results: law of large numbers, central limit theorem, Bayes' theorem. Important distributions: normal, binomial, Poisson, exponential. Probability underpins statistics, machine learning, quantum mechanics, finance, and risk assessment. Key theorems include Markov chains, the central limit theorem (explaining why the normal distribution appears everywhere), and the Poisson limit theorem for rare events.

title: Bayesian Statistics
body: Bayesian statistics interprets probability as belief updated via Bayes' theorem. Named after Thomas Bayes. Prior and likelihood produce posterior. Methods: MCMC, conjugate priors, hierarchical models. Provides credible intervals and incorporates prior knowledge. Applications: A/B testing, ML, drug discovery, spam filtering. Bayesian methods have grown dramatically with computational advances like Markov Chain Monte Carlo and variational inference, enabling complex hierarchical models and uncertainty quantification.

title: Set Theory
body: Set theory is the foundation of mathematics, developed by Cantor in the 1870s. Cantor proved reals are uncountable and established infinite cardinalities. Russell's paradox (1901) led to ZFC axiomatic system. Key concepts: cardinality, ordinals, axiom of choice, continuum hypothesis (undecidable per Cohen and Godel). Cantor's continuum hypothesis, stating no set has cardinality between integers and reals, was proved independent of ZFC by Godel (1940) and Cohen (1963) using forcing.

title: Game Theory
body: Game theory analyzes strategic interactions. Von Neumann and Morgenstern founded it in 1944. Nash proved every finite game has a Nash equilibrium. Concepts: zero-sum, prisoner's dilemma, mechanism design. Essential in economics, evolutionary biology, political science, and multi-agent AI. Nobel Memorial Prizes have been awarded for game theory contributions including Nash (1994), Harsanyi and Selten (1994), and Aumann and Schelling (2005). Mechanism design applies game theory to engineering economic systems.

title: Graph Theory
body: Graph theory studies vertices and edges. Euler's 1736 bridges solution was the first theorem. Concepts: trees, planar graphs, coloring, Hamiltonian/Eulerian paths. Major results: four-color theorem (1976, first computer-proved), Ramsey theory. Fundamental to CS, biology (protein networks), transportation. The four-color theorem was the first major mathematical theorem proved using a computer, sparking debate about the nature of mathematical proof.

title: Category Theory
body: Category theory provides an abstract framework via objects and morphisms. Developed by Eilenberg and Mac Lane in the 1940s. Concepts: functors, natural transformations, adjunctions, Yoneda's lemma. Underpins algebraic geometry, homological algebra, and functional programming (monads, functors in Haskell). In computer science, category theory provides the mathematical foundation for functional programming concepts like monads, functors, and applicatives in languages like Haskell and Scala.

title: Information Theory
body: Founded by Claude Shannon in 1948. Shannon entropy H=-sum p log p measures information. Source coding theorem (optimal compression) and noisy-channel coding theorem (max reliable rate). Foundation of telecommunications, data compression (ZIP, JPEG, MP3), cryptography, and ML (cross-entropy loss). Shannon's mathematical theory of communication established fundamental limits on data compression and reliable transmission that guide all modern communication system design.

title: Calculus
body: Developed by Newton and Leibniz in the 17th century. Differential calculus: rates of change via derivatives. Integral calculus: accumulation via integrals. The Fundamental Theorem connects them. Concepts: limits, Taylor series, multivariable calculus. Language of physics, engineering, economics, and ML (gradient descent). Newton and Leibniz independently developed calculus, sparking a priority dispute that divided European mathematics. Modern calculus underpins differential equations, optimization, and scientific computing.

title: Fourier Analysis
body: Fourier analysis decomposes functions into sinusoidal components. Fourier introduced the series in 1822 for heat equations. The Fourier transform converts time to frequency domains. FFT (Cooley-Tukey 1965) revolutionized signal processing. Essential in audio, JPEG, spectroscopy, quantum mechanics, and PDEs. The Laplace transform and wavelet transform extend Fourier analysis to handle transient signals and provide time-frequency localization, essential for modern signal processing and data analysis.

title: Partial Differential Equations
body: PDEs describe functions of multiple variables and partial derivatives. Types: elliptic (Laplace), parabolic (heat), hyperbolic (wave). Model electromagnetism (Maxwell), fluid dynamics (Navier-Stokes), quantum mechanics (Schrodinger). Numerical methods (finite elements) essential for solving them in practice. The Clay Mathematics Institute lists the Navier-Stokes existence and smoothness problem as one of its seven Millennium Prize Problems with a one million dollar prize.

title: Classical Mechanics
body: Describes macroscopic motion. Newton's three laws and universal gravitation (Principia 1687) form the foundation. Lagrangian mechanics (Lagrange 1788) uses energy; Hamiltonian mechanics (Hamilton 1833) uses phase space. Concepts: momentum, energy, harmonic oscillators. Accurate for everyday scales, breaks down at relativistic and quantum scales. Lagrangian and Hamiltonian formulations extend Newton's laws to generalized coordinates, enabling analysis of complex systems like planetary motion, rigid body dynamics, and coupled oscillators.

title: Newton's Laws of Motion
body: Three laws published in Principia (1687). First: inertia. Second: F=ma. Third: equal and opposite reaction. Combined with universal gravitation, they explain planetary orbits, projectiles, and tides. Foundation of mechanical engineering. These three laws, combined with the law of universal gravitation, successfully predicted the existence of Neptune before it was observed, a triumph of Newtonian mechanics.

title: Thermodynamics
body: Studies heat, work, temperature, energy. Four laws: zeroth (temperature equilibrium), first (energy conservation dU=Q-W), second (entropy never decreases, arrow of time), third (absolute zero unattainable). Concepts: enthalpy, Gibbs free energy, Carnot cycle. Developed by Carnot, Clausius, Boltzmann, Gibbs. Essential for engines and chemistry. The second law introduces entropy, a concept that extends far beyond thermodynamics to information theory, statistical mechanics, and the arrow of time in physics and cosmology.

title: Statistical Mechanics
body: Connects microscopic behavior to macroscopic thermodynamics via probability. Developed by Boltzmann, Gibbs, Maxwell. Temperature is average kinetic energy; entropy S=k ln W counts microstates. Key ensembles: microcanonical, canonical, grand canonical. The Boltzmann distribution gives state probabilities at given temperature. The partition function encapsulates all thermodynamic information about a system in equilibrium, enabling calculation of energy, entropy, and other macroscopic properties.

title: Electromagnetism
body: Unifies electricity, magnetism, light. Maxwell's four equations (1864) predict electromagnetic waves at light speed. Concepts: Coulomb's law, Gauss's law, Faraday's induction, Ampere's law, Lorentz force. Hertz confirmed EM waves in 1887. Governs electronics, optics, radio, and telecommunications. Maxwell's equations unified electricity, magnetism, and optics, predicting that light is an electromagnetic wave, which was later confirmed by Hertz's experiments with radio waves.

title: Quantum Mechanics
body: Describes nature at atomic scales. Developed 1900-1925 (Planck to Heisenberg/Schrodinger). Key concepts: wave-particle duality, quantization, uncertainty principle, superposition, entanglement. The most accurately tested theory. Enables transistors, LEDs, MRI, lasers, and quantum computing. Interpretations remain debated: the Copenhagen interpretation, many-worlds (Everett), pilot-wave (Bohm), and QBism offer different views of what quantum theory means.

title: Schrodinger Equation
body: Fundamental equation of quantum mechanics (1926): i hbar dPsi/dt = H Psi. The wave function contains probabilistic information via Born's rule. Time-independent: H Psi = E Psi. Exact solutions exist for hydrogen atom, harmonic oscillator. Multi-electron systems require approximation. The equation describes the quantum state evolution for non-relativistic particles. Dirac later developed a relativistic version that predicted the existence of antimatter.

title: Special Relativity
body: Einstein's 1905 theory: physics laws identical in all inertial frames; light speed c constant. Consequences: time dilation, length contraction, mass-energy equivalence E=mc^2. Lorentz transformations replace Galilean. Essential for particle physics and GPS corrections. Einstein's 1905 paper On the Electrodynamics of Moving Bodies resolved the conflict between Maxwell's equations and Newtonian mechanics, establishing a new spacetime framework.

title: General Relativity
body: Einstein's 1915 theory: gravity as spacetime curvature. Field equations relate geometry to energy-momentum. Predictions: light bending (1919), gravitational waves (LIGO 2015), black holes, time dilation. Essential for GPS, cosmology, and astrophysics. GR predicts black holes, gravitational waves, and the expanding universe. Its predictions have been confirmed with high precision, including by the first black hole image in 2019.

title: Quantum Field Theory
body: Combines QM with special relativity. Particles are field excitations. Developed by Dirac, Feynman, Schwinger, Tomonaga. The Standard Model is a QFT. Concepts: creation/annihilation operators, Feynman diagrams, renormalization, gauge symmetry. Predicts pair production, vacuum fluctuations, antiparticles. Feynman's path integral formulation provides an alternative to operator methods, representing quantum amplitudes as sums over all possible histories of a system.

title: Standard Model of Particle Physics
body: Describes three forces (electromagnetic, weak, strong) and all elementary particles. Developed 1960s-1970s. Particles: six quarks, six leptons, gauge bosons (photon, W, Z, gluons), Higgs (discovered 2012 at CERN). The Higgs mechanism gives mass. Missing: gravity, dark matter, neutrino masses. The Higgs boson discovery at CERN in 2012 completed the Standard Model particle spectrum. Both the ATLAS and CMS experiments confirmed the Higgs with 5-sigma significance.

title: Quantum Electrodynamics
body: QED is the quantum field theory of light and matter. The most accurately tested theory (one part in trillion for electron magnetic moment). Coupling constant alpha ~ 1/137. Developed by Feynman, Schwinger, Tomonaga, Dyson. Prototype for all quantum field theories. QED's remarkable precision comes from the smallness of the fine-structure constant alpha approx 1/137, making perturbative calculations highly accurate.

title: Condensed Matter Physics
body: Studies solids and liquids, the largest physics branch. Phenomena: superconductivity (1911), superfluidity, magnetism, quantum Hall effect. Band theory distinguishes metals, insulators, semiconductors. BCS theory explains conventional superconductivity. Enabled semiconductors, LEDs, solar cells. Landau's Fermi liquid theory describes interacting electrons in metals. Topological phases of matter represent a new frontier, with topological insulators discovered in the 2000s.

title: Optics
body: Studies light behavior. Geometrical optics: ray tracing. Physical optics: waves, interference, diffraction, polarization. Modern: lasers (1960), nonlinear optics, fiber optics, metamaterials, quantum optics. Essential instruments: microscope, telescope, spectrometer. Modern optics includes nonlinear phenomena like second-harmonic generation, optical parametric oscillation, and four-wave mixing, enabling laser frequency conversion and quantum optics experiments.

title: Atomic Physics
body: Studies isolated atoms. Bohr model (1913) explained hydrogen spectra. Quantum mechanics describes structure via orbitals, spin, Pauli principle. Laser cooling enables Bose-Einstein condensation (1995). Atomic clocks define the second with accuracy >10^-16. Atomic clocks use the hyperfine transition of cesium-133 at 9.2 GHz to define the second. Optical lattice clocks now achieve precision of one second in 15 billion years.

title: Nuclear Physics
body: Studies nuclei: protons and neutrons held by strong force. Radioactivity discovered by Becquerel (1896). Key concepts: binding energy, decay (alpha, beta, gamma), fission, fusion. Applications: nuclear power, PET, radiotherapy, carbon dating. The liquid drop model explains nuclear fission, while the shell model explains magic numbers and nuclear stability patterns analogous to electron shells in atoms.

title: Particle Physics
body: Studies fundamental constituents using accelerators like LHC at CERN. Key discoveries: top quark (1995), tau neutrino (2000), Higgs (2012). Unsolved: hierarchy problem, matter-antimatter asymmetry, dark matter, neutrino masses. The Large Hadron Collider at CERN accelerates protons to 6.5 TeV, the highest energy ever achieved by a particle accelerator. Its 27 km ring straddles the France-Switzerland border.

title: Cosmology
body: Studies universe origin and evolution. Big Bang theory supported by Hubble expansion, CMB (1965), nucleosynthesis. Universe is 13.8 billion years old: 5% matter, 27% dark matter, 68% dark energy (Lambda-CDM). Inflation (Guth 1980) explains flatness. JWST probes first galaxies. The James Webb Space Telescope, launched in 2021, observes the first galaxies formed less than 400 million years after the Big Bang, testing theories of galaxy formation.

title: Inorganic Chemistry
body: Studies non-carbon compounds. Key: coordination chemistry (transition metal complexes), crystal field theory, organometallics, solid-state chemistry. Werner founded coordination chemistry (Nobel 1913). Applications: catalysis (Haber-Bosch), batteries, pigments, perovskites for solar cells. Inorganic compounds include coordination complexes, metal clusters, and solid-state materials. They are vital in industrial catalysts, electronic materials, and pigments.

title: Organic Chemistry
body: Studies carbon compounds, basis of life and synthetic materials. Carbon's four bonds enable vast diversity. Concepts: functional groups, reaction mechanisms (SN1, SN2, E1, E2), stereochemistry, retrosynthesis. Systematized by Kekule, Butlerov. Enables pharmaceuticals, polymers, dyes. Carbon compounds form the basis of all known life. The field's development enabled the synthesis of complex natural products and pharmaceuticals, transforming medicine and materials science.

title: Physical Chemistry
body: Applies physics to chemical systems. Key areas: thermodynamics (Gibbs free energy), kinetics (rate laws), quantum chemistry (molecular orbitals), spectroscopy, statistical thermodynamics. Gibbs, Arrhenius, Pauling, Eyring. Bridges macroscopic observations to molecular behavior. Physical chemistry provides the theoretical framework for understanding reaction rates, molecular structure, and spectroscopic properties across all branches of chemistry.

title: Quantum Chemistry
body: Applies QM to molecules. The Schrodinger equation requires approximations for multi-electron systems. Methods: Hartree-Fock, configuration interaction, coupled cluster, DFT (Nobel 1998 to Kohn and Pople). Predicts geometries, energies, spectra. Essential for drug discovery and materials. Modern quantum chemistry software packages like Gaussian, VASP, and ORCA enable researchers to predict molecular properties before synthesis, accelerating drug discovery and materials design.

title: Analytical Chemistry
body: Identifies and quantifies substances. Classical: gravimetric, volumetric. Instrumental: spectroscopy (AAS, ICP-MS), chromatography (GC, HPLC, LC-MS), mass spec, NMR. Essential for pharma QC, environmental monitoring, diagnostics, forensic science. Detection down to parts-per-trillion. Modern analytical instruments can detect single molecules and quantify substances at parts-per-quadrillion concentrations, enabling advances in proteomics, metabolomics, and environmental monitoring.

title: Polymer Chemistry
body: Studies macromolecules of repeating monomers. Natural: proteins, cellulose, DNA. Synthetic: polyethylene, nylon, polystyrene. Polymerization: chain-growth and step-growth. Staudinger proposed macromolecular hypothesis (1920). Modern: conducting polymers, biodegradable, controlled radical polymerization. Polymers are ubiquitous in modern life, from packaging materials to high-performance engineering plastics, elastomers, adhesives, and biomedical implants. The synthesis of well-defined polymers with controlled architecture through ATRP, RAFT, and NMP has revolutionized materials science. Modern polymer research also explores conductive polymers for electronics, hydrogels for biomedical applications, and biodegradable alternatives to petroleum-based plastics.

title: Periodic Table
body: Arranges elements by atomic number and electron configuration. Mendeleev published the first version in 1869, predicting undiscovered elements. 118 elements in 7 periods, 18 groups. Trends: atomic radius decreases across periods, ionization energy increases, fluorine most electronegative. This knowledge entry provides essential factual information about the subject, including historical context, key developments, and significance in its field.

title: Solar System
body: The Sun (G-type star, 99.86% of mass), eight planets, five dwarf planets, moons, asteroids, comets. Inner rocky: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars. Outer gas giants: Jupiter, Saturn. Ice giants: Uranus, Neptune. Asteroid belt, Kuiper Belt, Oort cloud. Formed 4.6 billion years ago from protoplanetary disk. The solar system formed from gravitational collapse of a molecular cloud 4.6 billion years ago. Planetary migration models like the Nice model explain the current orbital configuration.

title: Stars and Stellar Evolution
body: Stars are plasma spheres sustained by fusion. Classification OBAFGKM by temperature. Low-mass stars become red giants then white dwarfs. High-mass stars (>8 solar masses) explode as supernovae, leaving neutron stars or black holes. HR diagram plots luminosity vs temperature. The most massive stars end as neutron stars or black holes, seeding the universe with heavy elements through supernova nucleosynthesis.

title: Galaxies
body: Massive systems of stars, gas, dust, dark matter bound by gravity. Hubble classification: elliptical (E0-E7), spiral (Sa-Sc, barred SB), irregular. Milky Way is barred spiral with ~100 billion stars. Supermassive black holes in centers. Galaxy clusters are largest bound structures. Galaxy formation follows hierarchical merging in the cold dark matter cosmology, with small galaxies merging to form larger ones over cosmic time.

title: Black Holes
body: Regions where gravity prevents light escape. Event horizon is the point of no return. Schwarzschild (non-rotating) and Kerr (rotating) types. Stellar-mass (3-100 solar masses) from supernovae. Supermassive (millions-billions solar masses) in galaxy centers. EHT imaged M87* in 2019. Hawking radiation predicts quantum evaporation. Gravitational wave observations by LIGO have detected dozens of black hole mergers, providing direct evidence for binary black hole systems.

title: Exoplanets
body: Planets outside our solar system. First confirmed in 1995 (51 Pegasi b). Detection: radial velocity, transit (Kepler discovered 2,600+), direct imaging, microlensing. Over 5,500 confirmed. Types: hot Jupiters, super-Earths, mini-Neptunes. JWST characterizes atmospheres for biosignatures. The Kepler space telescope (2009-2018) revolutionized exoplanet science, showing that most stars have planets and that Earth-sized planets are common.

title: Telescopes and Observatories
body: Collect electromagnetic radiation. Optical: refractors, reflectors. Major: Mauna Kea (Keck), Paranal (VLT), ELT (under construction). Space: Hubble (1990), JWST (2021), Chandra, Fermi. Radio: Green Bank, ALMA. JWST's 6.5m mirror observes first galaxies and exoplanet atmospheres. The Hubble Space Telescope has made over 1.5 million observations since 1990, fundamentally changing our understanding of the universe.

title: Big Bang Theory
body: Universe originated 13.8 billion years ago from hot dense state. Evidence: Hubble's law (receding galaxies), CMB (2.73K), nucleosynthesis (H/He/D/Li). Inflation solved horizon and flatness problems. Dark energy drives acceleration. Planck satellite (2013) confirmed Lambda-CDM. The Lambda-CDM model incorporating dark energy and cold dark matter successfully explains cosmic structure formation and the accelerated expansion of the universe.

title: Plate Tectonics
body: Describes Earth's lithosphere moving in plates, established 1960s. Seven major plates move 2-15 cm/year driven by mantle convection, slab pull, ridge push. Boundaries: divergent (mid-ocean ridges), convergent (subduction, mountains), transform (San Andreas). Explains earthquakes, volcanoes, continental arrangement. Unique to Earth. Plate tectonics explains the distribution of earthquakes, volcanoes, mountain ranges, and ocean basins. It is driven by mantle convection.

title: Earthquakes
body: Occur when accumulated strain releases along faults. Moment magnitude scale is logarithmic. Major: Valdivia 1960 (9.5, largest), Sumatra 2004 (9.1, 230K killed by tsunami), Tohoku 2011 (9.1, Fukushima). Seismic waves: P (fastest), S (shear), surface (most damaging). Early warning gives seconds of alert. Earthquake prediction remains elusive, but early warning systems using seismic networks provide crucial seconds of alert for populated areas.

title: Volcanoes
body: Vents where magma reaches the surface. Types: shield (gentle, basalt), stratovolcanoes (steep, explosive), cinder cones. At plate boundaries and hotspots (Hawaii, Yellowstone). Major: Krakatoa 1883, St. Helens 1980, Tambora 1815 (year without summer). Ring of Fire encircles Pacific. Volcanic eruptions can inject ash and sulfur dioxide into the stratosphere, affecting global climate for years as demonstrated by Pinatubo in 1991.

title: Rock Cycle
body: Transformation among three rock types. Igneous: from cooling magma (granite, basalt). Sedimentary: from accumulated sediment (sandstone, limestone). Metamorphic: from heat/pressure (slate, marble). Driven by plate tectonics and hydrologic cycle. Fundamental to geology and resource extraction. Metamorphic rocks record the pressure-temperature history of mountain building. Index minerals like garnet and kyanite indicate metamorphic grade.

title: Minerals and Crystals
body: Naturally occurring inorganic solids with ordered atomic structure. 5,000+ known minerals. Seven crystal systems. Mohs hardness (1-10). Silicates dominate crust. Ore minerals extracted for metals. Gemstones: diamond, ruby, sapphire. Formation: magma crystallization, hydrothermal precipitation, evaporation. Minerals are classified by their chemical composition and crystal structure. The silicate class dominates Earth's crust with feldspar and quartz being most abundant.

title: Geological Time Scale
body: Divides Earth's 4.54 billion year history. Eons: Hadean (magma), Archean (first life), Proterozoic (oxygen rise), Phanerozoic (visible life). Phanerozoic eras: Paleozoic (Cambrian explosion, Pangea), Mesozoic (dinosaurs), Cenozoic (mammals). Boundaries mark mass extinctions. Dating: radiometric (U-Pb, K-Ar, C-14). The ICS maintains the standardized time scale. The Anthropocene, a proposed new epoch defined by human impact, is under debate.

title: Tropical Rainforest Climate
body: Af type near equator (10N to 10S). Year-round 26-28C, >2000mm rainfall. Daily thunderstorms from intense solar heating. Amazon, Congo Basin, Indonesia largest regions. ITCZ drives rainfall. Supports the most biodiverse ecosystems. Deforestation threatens these climates. Tropical rainforests are critically important for global climate regulation through carbon storage, evapotranspiration, and cloud formation.

title: Desert Climate
body: BW type, <250mm annual precipitation. Hot deserts (Sahara, Arabian): 50C+, large diurnal swings. Cold deserts (Gobi): cold winters. Form at ~30 degrees latitude and rain shadows. Cover 33% of land. Adaptations: water storage (cacti), nocturnal behavior, deep roots. Desertification, driven by climate change and poor land management, threatens the livelihoods of over two billion people in dryland regions.

title: Tundra Climate
body: ET type: cold year-round, short cool summers (0-10C), long harsh winters. Permafrost underlies the active layer. Arctic regions and alpine zones. Precipitation <250mm. Vegetation: mosses, lichens. Climate change warms tundra, thawing permafrost releases methane/CO2. Permafrost contains roughly twice as much carbon as the atmosphere. Its thaw releases greenhouse gases in a dangerous climate feedback loop.

title: Taiga (Boreal Forest)
body: World's largest terrestrial biome, 11% of land (Canada, Scandinavia, Russia). Dfc climate: long cold winters, short mild summers. Dominant conifers: spruce, fir, pine, larch. Wildfires are natural disturbance. Stores ~30% of global terrestrial carbon. Climate change increases fire frequency. Boreal forests are experiencing increased pest outbreaks (mountain pine beetle), more frequent megafires, and permafrost thaw due to rapid Arctic warming.

title: Grassland Climate
body: 250-800mm precipitation, supports grasses. Temperate: steppe, prairie, pampas, veld. Tropical savannas: Serengeti, Cerrado, distinct wet/dry seasons. Fire maintains grasslands. Large herbivores (bison, wildebeest). Extensively converted to agriculture, highly endangered biome. Grasslands are the most endangered biome on Earth, with less than 5% of tallgrass prairie remaining in North America. Grasslands support some of the highest soil organic carbon levels of any ecosystem. The deep root systems of perennial grasses store carbon deep underground. Grassland conversion to cropland has released vast amounts of this stored carbon.

title: Mediterranean Climate
body: Csa/Csb: hot dry summers, mild wet winters at 30-45 degrees western continental sides. Five regions: Mediterranean Basin, California, Chile, Cape South Africa, SW Australia. Fire-adapted chaparral, fynbos. Densely populated, olives and grapes, facing water scarcity and increasing wildfire. Mediterranean climate regions are biodiversity hotspots with high endemism, but face severe threats from development, invasive species, and climate change.

title: Amazon Rainforest
body: ~5.5 million km2 across nine countries, half of remaining tropical rainforest. 390 billion trees of 16,000 species. Generates ~50% of its own rainfall. Deforestation ~17%, approaching dieback tipping (20-25%). Stores 150-200 billion tons carbon. Indigenous territories are most effective barriers. Tropical rainforests are critically important for global climate regulation through carbon storage, evapotranspiration, and cloud formation.

title: Coral Reef Ecosystems
body: Built by coral polyps in symbiosis with zooxanthellae. Great Barrier Reef (2,300 km) is largest living structure. Reefs occupy <0.1% of ocean but host 25% of marine species. Bleaching from heat stress. 2016-2017 GBR bleaching killed ~50% of shallow corals. Ocean acidification reduces calcification. Coral restoration through microfragmentation, larval reseeding, and assisted evolution of heat-tolerant corals offers hope for reef survival.

title: Ocean Ecosystems
body: Range from sunlit surface to deep-sea vents. Photic zone (upper 200m) supports phytoplankton, base of marine food webs. Coastal: coral reefs, kelp forests, mangroves. Deep sea (largest habitat on Earth), hydrothermal vents rely on chemosynthesis. Open ocean supports tuna, sharks, whales. Threats: overfishing, plastic pollution, acidification. The ocean has absorbed 90% of excess heat from global warming and 30% of anthropogenic CO2, driving sea-level rise and acidification.

title: Freshwater Ecosystems
body: Lakes, rivers, ponds, streams, wetlands. Lakes stratify thermally. Rivers transport nutrients. Wetlands are most productive ecosystems, providing flood control and water filtration. Freshwater holds 2.5% of Earth's water but hosts 10% of species. Biodiversity declining fastest due to dams, pollution, invasive species. Dams fragment 60% of the world's large rivers. Glacier melt threatens freshwater supplies for over a billion people in Asia and the Andes.

title: Polar Ecosystems
body: Arctic and Antarctic adapted to extreme cold, seasonal light extremes, ice cover. Arctic: tundra, sea ice, polar bears, walruses. Antarctic: krill (keystone species), penguins (emperor, adelie), seals, whales. Sea ice critical habitat rapidly declining. Arctic warming four times faster than global average. Polar amplification means the Arctic warms four times faster than the global average, with profound implications for global weather patterns.

title: Asia Geography
body: Largest continent (44.6 million km2, 30% of land), most populous (4.7 billion, 60% of world). Regions: East Asia (China, Japan, Korea), Southeast Asia, South Asia (India), Central Asia, Middle East. Himalayas tallest range (Everest 8,849m). Major rivers: Yangtze, Yellow, Ganges, Mekong. Economy exceeds all others combined by PPP. Asia contains the highest and lowest points on Earth, the most populous countries (China, India), and the largest economies by PPP.

title: Africa Geography
body: Second-largest (30.4 million km2), 1.4 billion people. Sahara Desert (9.2 million km2) dominates north. Rift Valley from Ethiopia to Mozambique. Nile (6,650 km) longest river. Immense mineral wealth: cobalt (DRC 70%), platinum, gold, diamonds. 54 countries, 3000+ languages. Youngest population (median ~19). Africa is the most genetically and linguistically diverse continent, containing the deepest branches of human evolutionary history.

title: North America Geography
body: 24.7 million km2. Canada, US, Mexico, Central America (7), Caribbean (13). Rockies from Alaska to New Mexico. Great Plains. Mississippi-Missouri (6,275 km). Great Lakes hold 21% of surface freshwater. Greenland largest island. Climate: Arctic to tropical. San Andreas Fault marks plate boundary. North America has the longest coastline of any continent and contains every climate zone from Arctic to tropical within its boundaries.

title: South America Geography
body: 17.8 million km2, 12 countries. Andes (7,000 km) along western edge. Amazon River largest by discharge. Amazon rainforest ~40% of continent. Atacama driest non-polar desert. Lake Titicaca highest navigable (3,812m). Angel Falls highest (979m). Rich in oil, copper, lithium, iron ore. South America contains the world's largest rainforest, driest desert, longest mountain range, and largest river by volume.

title: Europe Geography
body: 10.2 million km2, 40+ countries, ~750 million. Alps (Mont Blanc 4,809m). Rivers: Danube (2,850 km, 10 countries), Volga (3,530 km, longest). EU (27 countries) largest single market. Peninsulas: Iberian, Italian, Balkan, Scandinavian. Oldest median age (~42). Climate: Mediterranean to subarctic. Europe has the most indented coastline of any continent and the highest ratio of coastline to land area.

title: Australia and Oceania Geography
body: 8.5 million km2. Australia flattest and driest inhabited continent. Great Dividing Range east. Outback covers interior. Great Barrier Reef 2,300 km. New Zealand: alpine terrain, Southern Alps (Cook 3,724m). Pacific islands: Melanesia, Micronesia, Polynesia. Sea-level rise threatens island nations. Unique wildlife: kangaroos, koalas, platypus. The ocean has absorbed 90% of excess heat from global warming and 30% of anthropogenic CO2, driving sea-level rise and acidification.

title: Antarctica Geography
body: Fifth-largest continent (14.2 million km2), coldest, windiest, highest, driest. Ice sheet: 26.5 million km3, 61% of fresh water, 2.2 km avg thickness. No permanent population. Antarctic Treaty (1959) banned military activity and mining. Climate change warms peninsula, ice shelves collapse. Ozone hole slowly recovering. The Antarctic ice sheet contains enough water to raise global sea levels by 58 meters if fully melted. Its stability is a critical climate concern.

title: Pacific Ocean
body: Largest and deepest (165.2 million km2, 46% of water). Mariana Trench (Challenger Deep 10,994m) deepest point. Ring of Fire surrounds it. ENSO alternates El Nino/La Nina affecting global weather. Great Pacific Garbage Patch of accumulated plastic. Relatively unexplored. The ocean has absorbed 90% of excess heat from global warming and 30% of anthropogenic CO2, driving sea-level rise and acidification.

title: Atlantic Ocean
body: Second-largest (106.5 million km2), separates Americas from Europe/Africa. Mid-Atlantic Ridge runs its length. Thermohaline circulation (global conveyor belt) carries warm water north, sinks in North Atlantic. AMOC could weaken from climate change. Sargasso Sea hosts unique Sargassum ecosystem. The ocean has absorbed 90% of excess heat from global warming and 30% of anthropogenic CO2, driving sea-level rise and acidification.

title: Indian Ocean
body: Third-largest (70.6 million km2), bounded by Africa, Asia, Australia. Warmest ocean, drives Asian monsoon. Indian Ocean Dipole affects rainfall. Major shipping routes. 2004 tsunami (9.1 earthquake, 230K killed across 14 countries) one of deadliest natural disasters. Hosts Maldives, Madagascar biodiversity. The ocean has absorbed 90% of excess heat from global warming and 30% of anthropogenic CO2, driving sea-level rise and acidification.

title: Arctic Ocean
body: Smallest and shallowest (14.1 million km2). Sea ice cover grows and melts seasonally. September minimum declined from ~7 million km2 (1979) to ~3.5 million km2 (40%+ reduction). Warms four times faster than global average. Opening shipping routes (Northern Sea Route). Permafrost thaw releases methane. The ocean has absorbed 90% of excess heat from global warming and 30% of anthropogenic CO2, driving sea-level rise and acidification.

title: Southern Ocean
body: Surrounds Antarctica, defined by Antarctic Circumpolar Current (ACC), world's strongest current. Connects all three major oceans. Antarctic Bottom Water drives global deep circulation. Absorbs ~40% of anthropogenic CO2 and ~75% of excess heat. Krill supports whales, penguins, seals. The ocean has absorbed 90% of excess heat from global warming and 30% of anthropogenic CO2, driving sea-level rise and acidification.

title: Mesopotamia (Sumer)
body: World's first civilization (c. 4500-1900 BCE) between Tigris and Euphrates (modern Iraq). Sumerians invented cuneiform writing (~3200 BCE), wheel, plow, base-60 (60-minute hours). City-states: Ur, Uruk, Lagash. Epic of Gilgamesh oldest literature. Akkadian, Babylonian, Assyrian empires followed. Legacy: urban civilization, writing, codified law. Mesopotamian innovations in irrigation, writing, law, and urban organization provided the template for all subsequent civilizations in the region.

title: Ancient Egypt
body: Flourished along Nile for 3000+ years. Unified by Narmer ~3100 BCE. Old Kingdom built Great Pyramids. New Kingdom: Hatshepsut, Akhenaten, Tutankhamun, Ramesses II. Hieroglyphics deciphered via Rosetta Stone (1799). Advances: medicine, geometry, 365-day calendar, engineering. Mummification reflected afterlife beliefs. Ended with Roman annexation (30 BCE). Egypt's unique geography along the Nile, with predictable annual floods, enabled agricultural surplus supporting a complex hierarchical society.

title: Indus Valley Civilization
body: Harappan civilization (c. 3300-1300 BCE) in Pakistan and NW India. Remarkable urban planning: grid layouts, advanced drainage, standardized bricks. Covered ~1.5 million km2. Indus script (400+ symbols) remains undeciphered. Bronze working, cotton textiles. Decline ~1900 BCE likely from climate change weakening monsoons. The Indus civilization had sophisticated urban infrastructure including the world's first known urban sanitation systems.

title: Ancient China: Xia, Shang, Zhou
body: Chinese civilization began along Yellow River. Xia Dynasty (c. 2070-1600 BCE) described in texts. Shang Dynasty (c. 1600-1046 BCE) left oracle bones as first writing. Zhou Dynasty (1046-256 BCE) introduced Mandate of Heaven. Hundred Schools of Thought: Confucianism, Daoism, Legalism. Warring States period ended with Qin unification. Philosophical foundation of Chinese civilization.

title: Ancient Greece
body: (c. 800-146 BCE) Laid foundations of Western culture. City-states (polis): Athens (democracy ~508 BCE), Sparta (military oligarchy). Persian Wars (490-479 BCE): Marathon, Thermopylae, Salamis. Golden Age: Parthenon, Socrates, Plato, Aristotle, Aeschylus, Sophocles, Euripides. Alexander (356-323 BCE) conquered Persia, spreading Hellenistic culture. Greek city-states pioneered coinage, the alphabet, and the Olympic Games, institutions that continue to shape modern society.

title: Roman Empire
body: Republic (509 BCE) to empire (27 BCE) to fall (476 CE). Punic Wars (264-146 BCE) defeated Carthage. Augustus began Pax Romana (200 years peace). Peak under Trajan (5 million km2). Achievements: concrete, aqueducts, roads (400,000 km), Roman law, Latin literature. Spread Christianity. Values: engineering, law, administration. Western Empire fell 476 CE.

title: Persian Empire
body: Achaemenid Empire founded by Cyrus the Great (550 BCE). Under Darius I, stretched from India to Greece (5.5 million km2, 44% of world population). Cyrus freed Jews from Babylonian captivity. Organization: satrapies, Royal Road (2,700 km), standard currency. Alexander conquered 331 BCE but Persian culture continued through Parthian and Sassanian Empires.

title: Maya Civilization
body: Flourished in Mesoamerica (2000 BCE-1500 CE). Classic period (250-900 CE): 40+ city-states (Tikal, Palenque) with pyramids and observatories. Only fully developed pre-Columbian writing system. Invented zero independently. Complex calendars (Long Count, Tzolk'in, Haab'). Advanced astronomy. 9th century collapse from drought, deforestation, warfare. Maya mathematics used a vigesimal (base-20) system. Their Long Count calendar tracks time over 5,125-year cycles.

title: Inca Empire
body: Largest pre-Columbian American empire (Tawantinsuyu), centered in Andes. From Cusco, stretched 4,000 km covering 2 million km2. Founded by Pachacuti (1438-1471). Built Machu Picchu, Sacsayhuaman (cyclopean stone walls), 40,000 km of roads (Qhapaq Nan). Quipus for records. Pizarro conquered 1532-1572 exploiting civil war and disease. Inca statecraft included a highly efficient food redistribution system and a network of storehouses that could feed the population for years.

title: Medieval Europe
body: (c. 500-1500 CE). Early Middle Ages: feudalism, Charlemagne (crowned 800). High Middle Ages (1000-1300): Gothic cathedrals (Chartres, Notre Dame), universities (Bologna 1088, Paris, Oxford), scholasticism (Thomas Aquinas). Crusades (1096-1291). Magna Carta (1215). Black Death (1347-1351) killed 30-50%, transforming labor. Hundred Years' War (1337-1453). Medieval guilds regulated trades and trained apprentices. The three-field crop rotation system improved agricultural productivity.

title: Islamic Golden Age
body: (c. 750-1258 CE) Extraordinary flourishing under Abbasids, centered on Baghdad's House of Wisdom. Key figures: Al-Khwarizmi (algebra), Ibn Sina/Avicenna (Canon of Medicine), Alhazen (optics, scientific method), Al-Biruni (astronomy), Al-Razi (smallpox), Ibn Rushd/Averroes. Advances in math, astronomy, medicine, chemistry. Cordoba was Europe's largest city. Ended with Mongol sack of Baghdad (1258).

title: Mongol Empire
body: Largest contiguous land empire, founded by Genghis Khan (c. 1162-1227). Under Kublai Khan spanned 24 million km2 from Korea to Hungary. Meritocracy, codified law (Yassa), religious tolerance. Superior cavalry with composite bows. Splintered into Yuan (China), Golden Horde (Russia), Ilkhanate (Persia), Chagatai. Pax Mongolica enabled Silk Road exchange. The Mongol Empire facilitated unprecedented cultural exchange across Eurasia, spreading technologies like papermaking and gunpowder from China to Europe.

title: Byzantine Empire
body: Eastern Roman Empire (330-1453 CE). Justinian I (527-565) reconquered Italy/North Africa, codified Roman law (Corpus Juris Civilis), built Hagia Sophia. Preserved Greek/Roman knowledge. Great Schism (1054) split Christianity. Fourth Crusade sacked Constantinople (1204). Achievements: Greek fire, Orthodox Christianity, Cyrillic alphabet, preserving ancient texts sparking Renaissance. Byzantine missionaries Cyril and Methodius created the Glagolitic alphabet, ancestor of the Cyrillic script used by many Slavic languages today.

title: Renaissance
body: Cultural rebirth (14th-17th centuries) starting in Florence. Humanism revived classical learning. Key: Leonardo (Mona Lisa, Last Supper), Michelangelo (David, Sistine Chapel), Raphael (School of Athens). Printing press (Gutenberg 1450) revolutionized knowledge. Science: Copernicus, Vesalius, Galileo. Medici family as patrons. Spread north (van Eyck, Durer, Bruegel) and to England (Shakespeare). The Renaissance saw the birth of modern banking, with the Medici family developing double-entry bookkeeping, letters of credit, and other financial innovations.

title: Age of Discovery
body: European global exploration (15th-17th centuries). Portugal: Prince Henry Navigator, Vasco da Gama to India (1498). Spain: Columbus to Americas (1492), Magellan circumnavigation. Cortes conquered Aztecs, Pizarro Incas. Treaty of Tordesillas (1494) divided world. Columbian Exchange: crops, diseases (smallpox killed 90% Native Americans), enslaved people. Created global trade. European exploration connected previously isolated world regions, initiating global trade networks that reshaped economies, ecologies, and societies.

title: Scientific Revolution
body: (1543-1687) Transformed understanding of nature. Copernicus proposed heliocentrism. Kepler's laws: elliptical orbits. Galileo: telescopic discoveries, experimental physics. Newton's Principia unified mechanics with universal gravitation. Bacon (empirical method), Descartes (rationalism), Boyle (chemistry), Harvey (circulation). Royal Society (1660) institutionalized science. The Royal Society's motto Nullius in Verba (take nobody's word for it) encapsulated the new emphasis on experimental verification over ancient authority.

title: French Revolution
body: (1789-1799) Overthrew absolute monarchy. Causes: Enlightenment ideas, fiscal crisis, inequality (three estates). Bastille fell July 14, 1789. Declaration of Rights of Man. Reign of Terror (1793-1794, Robespierre) executed 40,000+ including Louis XVI. Napoleon's coup (1799) ended it. Impacts: nationalism, secular states, metric system, end of feudalism. The French Revolution established the metric system, abolished feudalism, and introduced the concept of universal human rights.

title: Industrial Revolution
body: (c. 1760-1840) Transformed manufacturing. Began in Britain: coal, steam, iron, textiles. Watt's steam engine (1765), spinning jenny, power loom, Bessemer steel. Railways revolutionized transport. Factory system, urbanization. Social: slums, child labor, labor unions, Marxism. Second Industrial Revolution (1870-1914): electricity, chemicals, oil, combustion engine. Increased living standards 10x. The Indus civilization had sophisticated urban infrastructure including the world's first known urban sanitation systems.

title: World War I
body: (1914-1918) 30+ nations, 70 million soldiers. Triggered by Franz Ferdinand assassination. Western Front: trench warfare, Verdun (700K casualties), Somme (1 million). New weapons: machine guns, poison gas, tanks, aircraft, submarines. US entry (1917) decisive. 20 million dead. Treaty of Versailles harsh on Germany. League of Nations failed. WWI introduced industrialized warfare including aerial combat and chemical weapons. It led to the collapse of four empires and redrew the global map.

title: World War II
body: (1939-1945) Deadliest conflict: 70-85 million dead. Germany invaded Poland Sept 1, 1939. Axis conquered before turning points: Britain (1940), Stalingrad (1942-43), Midway (1942), D-Day (1944). Holocaust: 6 million Jews murdered. US dropped atomic bombs on Hiroshima (Aug 6) and Nagasaki (Aug 9, 1945). UN founded, Cold War began, nuclear weapons defined geopolitics.

title: Cold War
body: (1947-1991) US-led NATO vs USSR-led Warsaw Pact. Proxy wars, arms race, ideological competition. Key: Berlin Blockade (1948), Korean War, Cuban Missile Crisis (1962, closest to nuclear war), Vietnam War, Space Race. Detente then renewed tension. Gorbachev's reforms (glasnost, perestroika) led to Berlin Wall fall (Nov 1989) and Soviet dissolution (Dec 1991).

title: Algorithms and Data Structures
body: Step-by-step procedures for computation. Key structures: arrays, linked lists, trees, hash tables, graphs. Sorting: quicksort O(n log n), mergesort, heapsort. Search: binary search O(log n), BFS, DFS. Graph: Dijkstra, A*, Kruskal, Prim. Dynamic programming. Big O notation. Core of computer science. Algorithm analysis was formalized by Donald Knuth in The Art of Computer Programming. Big O notation was introduced by Paul Bachmann and popularized by Knuth.

title: Artificial Intelligence
body: Creating systems exhibiting intelligent behavior. Early AI (1950s-1970s): symbolic reasoning, expert systems. ML (1980s-2000s): neural networks, SVMs. Deep learning (2010s): CNNs (vision), RNNs (sequence), transformers (NLP). Reinforcement learning: AlphaGo. Generative AI: GPT, DALL-E. Challenges: alignment, reasoning, robustness. Turing's 1950 paper Computing Machinery and Intelligence proposed the Imitation Game (Turing Test) as a criterion for machine intelligence.

title: Programming Languages
body: Formal systems for instructing computers. Generation: machine code to high-level (C, Java, Python, JavaScript). Paradigms: imperative, OO, functional, declarative. Type systems: static vs dynamic, strong vs weak. Memory: manual (C), GC (Java), ownership (Rust). Each paradigm shapes how programmers solve problems. The first high-level programming language, Plankalkul, was designed by Konrad Zuse in the 1940s. FORTRAN (1957) was the first widely used high-level language.

title: Computer Networks
body: Connect devices for data exchange. OSI model (7 layers) and TCP/IP (4 layers). Key protocols: IP, TCP, UDP, HTTP/HTTPS, DNS, DHCP. Topologies: star, mesh, bus. Ethernet dominates LAN. Internet interconnects globally. Packet switching. IPv4 and IPv6. Network security: firewalls, TLS/SSL, VPNs. ARPANET, the precursor to the Internet, began in 1969 with four nodes. TCP/IP became the standard protocol in 1983, marking the birth of the modern Internet.

title: Operating Systems
body: Manages hardware and provides services. Functions: process management, memory (virtual, paging), file systems (NTFS, ext4, APFS), device drivers, security. Major: Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS. The kernel (monolithic vs microkernel). Processes vs threads, context switching, deadlocks, interrupt handling. Unix, developed at Bell Labs in 1969, became the model for most modern operating systems including Linux, macOS, and Android.

title: Database Systems
body: Store, retrieve, manage structured data. Relational (RDBMS): MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL. ACID properties. NoSQL: document (MongoDB), key-value (Redis), column-family (Cassandra), graph (Neo4j). Indexing (B-trees). Normalization, query optimization, concurrency control, replication. CAP theorem for distributed databases. Edgar Codd proposed the relational model in 1970, transforming data management. SQL became the standard query language.

title: Machine Learning
body: Systems that learn from data. Supervised: regression, classification. Algorithms: linear regression, decision trees, random forests, SVMs, neural nets. Unsupervised: clustering (K-means), PCA. Evaluation: cross-validation, precision/recall/F1. Overfitting vs underfitting, bias-variance tradeoff. MLOps manages lifecycle. Machine learning evolved from pattern recognition and artificial intelligence research. The term was coined by Arthur Samuel in 1959.

title: Deep Learning
body: Multi-layer neural networks learning hierarchical representations. CNNs for images. RNNs/LSTMs for sequences. Transformers (self-attention) for NLP. Training: backpropagation, Adam. LLMs (GPT-4, Claude) with billions of params. Generative: GANs, VAEs, diffusion. GPU/TPU accelerated. Applications: vision, NLP, speech, drug discovery. The 2012 AlexNet breakthrough in image recognition sparked the deep learning revolution. Transformer architecture (2017) revolutionized NLP.

title: Cybersecurity
body: Protects systems from digital attacks. Principles: confidentiality (encryption), integrity (hashing), availability. Threats: malware, phishing, DDoS, SQL injection, zero-day. Defense: firewalls, IDS, antivirus, AES/RSA/TLS, MFA, patches. CIA triad. Incident response. Zero Trust architecture. The first computer virus (Creeper) appeared in 1971. Modern cybersecurity faces sophisticated threats from state-sponsored APT groups and organized cybercrime.

title: Computer Architecture
body: Structure of computer systems. Von Neumann: CPU, memory, I/O. CPU: control unit, ALU, registers. Pipeline, cache (L1-L3). RISC (ARM) vs CISC (x86). Parallelism: multi-core, SIMD. Memory hierarchy: registers to disk. Moore's Law driving progress, now slowing. Billions of transistors on modern chips. Modern CPUs contain billions of transistors. Moore's Law has driven exponential improvement since 1965 but is now slowing as transistors approach atomic scales.

title: Human Anatomy
body: Structure of the human body. Hierarchical: cells, tissues, organs, systems. 11 systems: skeletal (206 bones), muscular (600+ muscles), nervous (86 billion neurons), circulatory, respiratory, digestive, endocrine, immune, urinary, reproductive, integumentary. Anatomy is foundational to medicine, surgery, and health sciences. Andreas Vesalius's De Humani Corporis Fabrica (1543) revolutionized anatomy, correcting errors in Galen's ancient texts through direct human dissection.

title: Cardiovascular System
body: Heart beats ~100,000 times daily, pumping 7,500 liters through 100,000 km of vessels. Cardiac cycle: systole/diastole. Blood pressure normal ~120/80. Diseases: coronary artery disease (leading death cause), heart attack, stroke, heart failure. Risk factors: smoking, hypertension, cholesterol, diabetes, obesity. Treatments: statins, beta-blockers, stents, bypass. Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of death globally, responsible for approximately 32% of all deaths according to the WHO.

title: Infectious Diseases
body: Caused by pathogens: bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites. Bacterial: TB (1.5M deaths/year), cholera. Viral: HIV/AIDS (40M deaths), influenza (500K/year), COVID-19 (7M+). Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is growing crisis. Vaccination most effective: smallpox eradicated (1980), polio nearly. Germ theory (Pasteur, Koch). Pandemics shaped history. The germ theory of disease transformed medicine, leading to antisepsis, vaccination, and antibiotics that doubled human life expectancy.

title: Public Health
body: Prevents disease at population level. Functions: disease surveillance, epidemiology, health promotion, environmental health, policy. Achievements: sanitation (doubled life expectancy), vaccination, tobacco control, workplace safety. Life expectancy rose from ~47 (1950) to ~73 (2023). Epidemiological transition: chronic diseases now leading causes. Health disparities remain stark. The WHO defines health as complete physical, mental and social well-being. Universal health coverage is a key UN Sustainable Development Goal.

title: Pharmacology and Drugs
body: How drugs interact with biological systems. Development: discovery, preclinical, clinical trials (Phase I-III), FDA approval. Classes: antibiotics, antivirals, statins, beta-blockers, SSRIs, opioids, NSAIDs, anticancer drugs. Pharmacokinetics (ADME) and pharmacodynamics. Adverse drug reactions. Personalized medicine using genetic information. Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928, ushering in the antibiotic era. Antimicrobial resistance now threatens to reverse these gains.

title: Civil Engineering
body: Designs infrastructure: buildings, bridges, roads, dams, tunnels, water systems. Sub-disciplines: structural, geotechnical, transportation, environmental, water resources. Principles: structural analysis, materials (concrete, steel), fluid mechanics. Landmarks: Hoover Dam, Channel Tunnel, Burj Khalifa (828m). Emphasizes safety, sustainability, resilience. Civil engineering is one of the oldest engineering disciplines. Megaprojects like the Panama Canal and Three Gorges Dam demonstrate its scale.

title: Mechanical Engineering
body: Designs mechanical systems, broadest engineering discipline. Core: mechanics (statics, dynamics, fluids), thermodynamics, materials, manufacturing. Applications: engines, HVAC, robotics, automotive, power generation. CAD and FEA essential tools. Landmarks: steam engine, automobile, jet engine, hard disk drive. Mechanical engineering principles underpin all transportation systems, from bicycles to spacecraft, and power generation from steam turbines to wind turbines.

title: Electrical Engineering
body: Deals with electricity, electronics, electromagnetism. Sub-disciplines: power systems, electronics, control, signal processing, telecommunications. Key inventions: transistor (1947, fundamental), integrated circuit, microprocessor. Power grid: generation, transmission, distribution. Electronics: analog/digital, op-amps, logic gates. Underlies all modern technology. The invention of the transistor in 1947 at Bell Labs is widely considered the most important invention of the 20th century.

title: Aerospace Engineering
body: Designs aircraft and spacecraft. Aeronautics: aerodynamics, propulsion, structures, flight controls. Astronautics: rocket propulsion, orbital mechanics, spacecraft design. Achievements: Wright Flyer (1903), Boeing 747, Apollo Moon landings, Concorde, Space Shuttle, ISS, Mars rovers. Modern focus: efficiency, composites, reusable rockets (SpaceX). The first powered flight by the Wright brothers in 1903 lasted 12 seconds. Commercial aviation now carries over 4 billion passengers annually.

title: Chemical Engineering
body: Applies chemistry to industrial processes. Core: mass/energy balances, thermodynamics, fluid dynamics, reaction engineering, separation processes. Designs refineries, chemical plants, pharmaceuticals, food processing. Contributions: Haber-Bosch (synthetic fertilizer, feeding billions), polymers. Challenges: carbon capture, circular economy. Unit operations, formalized by Arthur D. Little in 1915, provide the building blocks of chemical processes and are essential for scale-up from lab to industry.

title: Biotechnology
body: Uses living organisms to develop products. Genetic engineering: recombinant DNA, CRISPR (Nobel 2020). Applications: pharmaceuticals (insulin in bacteria since 1982), GMO crops, mRNA vaccines, biofuels, bioremediation. DNA sequencing cost dropped from $3 billion (Human Genome Project) to under $1,000. Synthetic biology designs novel biological systems. Monoclonal antibodies are among the most successful biotech products, generating over $100 billion annually and treating cancer and autoimmune diseases.

title: Materials Science
body: Studies structure-property relationships. Categories: metals, ceramics, polymers, composites, semiconductors, biomaterials. Key properties: strength, toughness, hardness, conductivity. Characterization: SEM, TEM, XRD. Frontiers: high-entropy alloys, 2D materials (graphene, Nobel 2004), metamaterials, shape memory alloys. The materials genome initiative aims to accelerate materials discovery through high-throughput computational screening and machine learning. The field combines solid-state physics, chemistry, and engineering. Computational materials science using DFT and machine learning accelerates discovery of new materials for batteries, catalysts, and structural applications.

title: Nanotechnology
body: Manipulates matter at 1-100 nm scale. Key materials: carbon nanotubes (100x stronger than steel), graphene, quantum dots, nanoparticles. Applications: targeted drug delivery, electronics (3nm transistors), solar cells, batteries, water filtration, sensors. Quantum effects dominate at nanoscale. Concerns: nanoparticle toxicity. Feynman's 1959 talk There's Plenty of Room at the Bottom envisioned nanotechnology. The field has delivered stain-resistant fabrics, targeted drug delivery, and stronger materials.

title: Socrates
body: (470-399 BCE) Developed Socratic method (elenchus) of questioning to expose contradictions. Wrote nothing; teachings from Plato. Sentenced to death for corrupting youth, drank hemlock. Focus on ethics (how should one live?). Know thyself and unexamined life not worth living. Foundational to all Western philosophy. The Socratic paradox I know that I know nothing captures his humble wisdom about human knowledge. His method shaped education for two millennia.

title: Plato
body: (428-348 BCE) Founded Academy. Theory of Forms: eternal abstract entities are true reality, physical world is shadow. The Republic: justice, ideal state, allegory of the cave. Symposium: love as ascent to beauty. Dualistic (body vs soul). Influenced Christian theology (Augustine), Neoplatonism, all Western thought. Plato founded the Academy in Athens, the first institution of higher learning in the Western world, which operated for over 900 years.

title: Aristotle
body: (384-322 BCE) Student of Plato, founded Lyceum. Rejected Forms for empiricism. Works cover logic (syllogism), metaphysics, ethics (golden mean, eudaimonia), politics (man is political animal), physics, biology, poetics, rhetoric. Four causes explain change. Influenced Islamic, Jewish, Christian philosophy for 1500 years. Aristotle's Lyceum conducted systematic research across all known fields. His ethical concept of the golden mean remains influential in virtue ethics.

title: Medieval Philosophy
body: (c. 400-1400 CE) Synthesized classical philosophy with theology. Augustine: City of God, problem of evil. Anselm: ontological argument. Aquinas (1225-1274): five proofs of God, natural law. Duns Scotus: voluntarism. Ockham: Ockham's razor. Arabic philosophy (Avicenna, Averroes) preserved Greek thought. Medieval guilds regulated trades and trained apprentices. The three-field crop rotation system improved agricultural productivity.

title: Descartes to Hume
body: Modern philosophy (17th-18th centuries) shifted to epistemology. Descartes: cogito ergo sum, mind-body dualism. Spinoza: pantheistic monism, determinism. Leibniz: monads, best of all possible worlds. Locke: tabula rasa. Berkeley: esse est percipi. Hume: skepticism (causation, induction, self). Kant synthesized both traditions. Cartesian dualism defines the modern mind-body problem. Descartes also invented Cartesian coordinates, linking algebra and geometry.

title: Immanuel Kant
body: (1724-1804) Critique of Pure Reason: mind structures experience through a priori categories (space, time, causality). Noumena unknowable. Categorical imperative: act by universalizable maxims, treat persons as ends. Critique of Judgment: aesthetics, teleology. Kant's ethics grounds human dignity and rights. Influenced German Idealism, existentialism, contemporary ethics. Kant's categorical imperative provides a foundation for human rights law and modern deontological ethics, arguing rational beings are ends in themselves.

title: Hegel
body: (1770-1831) Absolute idealism: reality is Geist self-unfolding through dialectic (thesis-antithesis-synthesis). Phenomenology of Spirit: master-slave dialectic, unhappy consciousness. History as progression of freedom. Aufhebung (sublation). Ethical life (Sittlichkeit). Influenced Marx, existentialism, pragmatism, modern political thought. Hegel's dialectic influenced Marx's historical materialism and remains influential in critical theory and contemporary political philosophy.

title: Nietzsche
body: (1844-1900) God is dead, attacked conventional morality as slave morality. Thus Spoke Zarathustra: Ubermensch creates values, eternal recurrence. Beyond Good and Evil: truth as metaphor. Will to power as fundamental drive. Apollonian vs Dionysian. Perspectivalism. Influenced existentialism, postmodernism, psychoanalysis. Nietzsche's critique of objective truth and morality anticipated 20th century postmodernism. His aphoristic style is unique among major philosophers.

title: 20th Century Continental Philosophy
body: Includes phenomenology, existentialism, hermeneutics, critical theory, post-structuralism. Husserl: phenomenology. Heidegger: Dasein, being-toward-death. Sartre: radical freedom, bad faith. Merleau-Ponty: embodiment. Levinas: ethics as first philosophy. Derrida: deconstruction. Foucault: power/knowledge. Deleuze: difference. Habermas: communicative action. Continental philosophy contrasts with analytic philosophy in its emphasis on historical context, lived experience, and engagement with culture and politics.

title: Hinduism
body: Oldest major religion, no single founder. Vedas (Rigveda c. 1500 BCE), Upanishads, Ramayana, Mahabharata (Bhagavad Gita). Core: dharma, karma, samsara, moksha, atman. Trimurti: Brahma, Vishnu, Shiva. Traditions: Vaishnavism, Shaivism, Shaktism. ~1.2 billion followers, third-largest religion. Yoga originated as Hindu spiritual practice. Hinduism's influence extends beyond religion to Indian philosophy, art, architecture, music, dance, and social structure.

title: Buddhism
body: Founded by Siddhartha Gautama (Buddha, c. 5th century BCE) in India. Four Noble Truths: suffering, craving causes it, it can end, Eightfold Path leads to nirvana. Middle Way. Theravada (oldest, Southeast Asia) and Mahayana (compassion, East Asia). Vajrayana (Tibetan). Concepts: anatta (no self), anicca (impermanence). Spread via Silk Road. ~500 million followers.

title: Confucianism
body: Founded by Confucius (551-479 BCE). Core: ren (benevolence), li (ritual propriety), xiao (filial piety). Five Relationships with mutual obligations. Analects. Mencius: human nature good. Xunzi: needs shaping. Emphasizes education, meritocracy. Official ideology of Chinese imperial exams for 2000 years. Still influential in China, Korea, Japan, Vietnam. Confucian values of filial piety and education continue to shape East Asian educational systems, corporate culture, and family structures.

title: Daoism (Taoism)
body: Based on Dao De Jing (Laozi, c. 6th century) and Zhuangzi. Dao is ineffable ultimate principle. Concepts: wu wei (effortless action), pu (simplicity), ziran (spontaneity), yin-yang. Daodejing: 81 short poetic chapters. Zhuangzi: butterfly dream. Influenced Chinese painting, poetry, martial arts (Taijiquan), medicine. One of Three Teachings. The concept of yin-yang has influenced Chinese medicine (balance of hot/cold, yin/yang), feng shui, and traditional philosophy for over two thousand years.

title: Islamic Philosophy
body: Developed from 9th century synthesizing Greek and Islamic thought. Al-Kindi introduced Greek philosophy. Al-Farabi: political philosophy. Avicenna (Ibn Sina): metaphysics (essence vs existence), logic. Al-Ghazali: The Incoherence of the Philosophers. Averroes (Ibn Rushd): Aristotle commentaries, defended philosophy. Ibn Arabi: unity of being. Mulla Sadra: transcendent theosophy. The Islamic world preserved and expanded classical knowledge during Europe's Dark Ages, developing algebra, chemistry, and advanced medical practices.

title: Empiricism vs Rationalism
body: Central epistemological debate. Empiricism (Locke, Berkeley, Hume): knowledge from sensory experience, mind as tabula rasa. Rationalism (Descartes, Spinoza, Leibniz): reason as primary source, innate ideas. Kant synthesized: mind structures experience via a priori categories. Shaped all subsequent epistemology and philosophy of science. Hume's problem of induction remains a central challenge for philosophy of science: how can we justify reasoning from past to future?

title: Marxism
body: Karl Marx (1818-1883) and Engels. Historical materialism: economic base determines superstructure. Class struggle drives history. Capital: commodities, surplus value, exploitation, alienation, crisis tendencies. Predicted proletarian revolution leading to communism. Influenced 20th century politics, economics, sociology. Critical Theory (Frankfurt School) updated Marx. Marxist analysis of capitalism as a crisis-prone system influenced 20th century revolutionary movements, labor unions, and academic disciplines.

title: Existentialism
body: Emphasizes freedom and choice. Kierkegaard: subjective truth, leap of faith, three stages. Nietzsche: will to power, Ubermensch, eternal recurrence. Heidegger: Dasein, authenticity. Sartre: existence precedes essence, condemned to be free, bad faith. Camus: the absurd. De Beauvoir: feminist existentialism. Influenced literature, psychology, political thought. Existentialist themes of freedom, authenticity, and responsibility resonate especially strongly in modern literature, film, and psychotherapy.

title: Pragmatism
body: American philosophy: truth lies in practical consequences. Peirce originated it. James popularized: truth is what works. Varieties of Religious Experience. Dewey: education (learning by doing), democracy, ethics. Rorty: neo-pragmatism. Influenced functional psychology, legal realism. Pragmatism's focus on consequences as the test of truth influenced American legal philosophy (legal realism) and continues to shape policy analysis.

title: Analytic Philosophy
body: Emphasizes clarity, logic, language analysis. Frege: modern logic. Russell: logical atomism, theory of descriptions. Wittgenstein: early (picture theory), later (language games, meaning as use). Vienna Circle: logical positivism (verification principle). Quine: naturalized epistemology. Kripke: modal logic, necessary a posteriori. Dominates English-speaking philosophy. Analytic philosophy's emphasis on logical clarity and argumentative rigor has set the standard for philosophical methodology in the English-speaking world.

title: Phenomenology
body: Studies conscious experience from first-person perspective. Husserl: epochē (bracketing assumptions), intentionality. Heidegger: hermeneutic phenomenology, Dasein's being-in-the-world. Merleau-Ponty: embodied perception. Sartre: existential phenomenology. Levinas: ethics as first philosophy. Influenced cognitive science (embodied cognition), social science, psychiatry. Phenomenological methods have been productively applied in cognitive science, psychiatry (understanding schizophrenia), and social theory.

title: Homer: Iliad and Odyssey
body: (c. 750 BCE) Foundational Western literature. Iliad: Achilles' wrath, Troy, honor (time), mortality. Odyssey: Odysseus's 10-year journey home, Cyclops, Circe, themes of cunning (metis) and homecoming (nostos). Oral composition in hexameter. Shaped Greek education and values. Influenced literature for 2700 years. The Homeric epics were transmitted orally for centuries before being written down. They remain the foundation of the Western literary canon.

title: Dante: Divine Comedy
body: (c. 1321) Supreme poem in terza rima. Dante journeys through Hell (Inferno), Purgatory (Purgatorio), Paradise (Paradiso). Virgil guides through Hell/Purgatory; Beatrice through Paradise. Synthesizes Catholic theology, classical mythology, Florentine politics. Nine circles of Hell. Written in Tuscan Italian. Explores sin, redemption, love, God. Dante's work established Tuscan Italian as the literary language of Italy, influencing every subsequent Italian writer.

title: Shakespeare
body: (1564-1616) Greatest English writer. 39 plays, 154 sonnets. Comedies: Midsummer Night's Dream, Twelfth Night. Histories: Henry IV, Richard III. Tragedies: Hamlet, King Lear, Macbeth, Othello. Sonnets: love, time, mortality. Innovations: soliloquy, mixing tragic/comic, ambiguous characters. Coined 1700+ English words. Performed globally. Shakespeare's works have been translated into over 100 languages and are performed more frequently than any other playwright.

title: Goethe: Faust
body: (Part I 1808, Part II 1832) Masterpiece of German literature. Faust makes pact with Mephistopheles for worldly experience. Part I: Gretchen tragedy. Part II: allegorical journey through classical/medieval worlds. Themes: ambition, knowledge, redemption. Constant striving earns divine grace. Composed over 60 years. Influenced German Romanticism. Goethe was also a scientist who made contributions to anatomy (discovering the intermaxillary bone), botany, and color theory.

title: Dostoevsky
body: (1821-1881) Russian psychological novelist. Crime and Punishment: Raskolnikov's murder and redemption. The Idiot: Christ-like Myshkin. Demons: revolutionary nihilism. The Brothers Karamazov: faith, doubt, free will, patricide, Grand Inquisitor. Themes: suffering, redemption, irrational depths, faith vs reason. Anticipated Freud. Dostoevsky's experience in a Siberian prison camp shaped his exploration of suffering, redemption, and the human capacity for evil and good.

title: Dream of the Red Chamber
body: Cao Xueqin (1791) Greatest of China's Four Great Classical Novels. Rise and fall of Jia clan through Baoyu. Love triangle: Daiyu (soulmate) vs Baochai (virtuous). 400+ characters, psychological depth, Qing dynasty elite life. Last 40 chapters traditionally by Gao E. Themes: love, fate, impermanence. Redology is dedicated scholarly field. The novel is studied extensively in China as a window into Qing dynasty culture, language, and social structure through its detailed portrayal of aristocratic life.

title: Romance of the Three Kingdoms
body: Luo Guanzhong (14th century) One of China's Four Great Classical Novels. 120 chapters dramatizing 184-280 CE. 1,000+ characters: Guan Yu (righteous), Zhuge Liang (strategist), Cao Cao (ruthless). Peach Garden Oath, Battle of Red Cliffs. Explores loyalty, strategy (36 Stratagems), Mandate of Heaven. Profoundly influenced Chinese culture. Roman administration and legal concepts fundamentally shaped European political institutions. Latin influenced all Romance languages.

title: James Joyce: Ulysses
body: (1922) Landmark modernist novel. Single day (June 16, 1904) in Dublin mirroring Homer's Odyssey. 18 episodes, each different style: stream of consciousness (Molly Bloom monologue), parody, newspaper headlines. Banned in US until 1933. Linguistic innovation, encyclopedic range. Bloomsday celebrated annually worldwide. Ulysses was banned in the US until Judge John M. Woolsey's landmark 1933 ruling that it was not obscene, a victory for literary freedom.

title: Franz Kafka
body: (1883-1924) Novels synonymous with existential anxiety and absurd bureaucracy. The Trial: K. arrested in opaque legal system. The Castle: struggle with inaccessible authorities. The Metamorphosis: waking as insect. Directed Brod to burn manuscripts; Brod published posthumously. Kafkaesque. Influenced existentialism, magic realism, postmodernism. Kafka's works have been adapted into numerous films, graphic novels, and operas, and the adjective Kafkaesque is used across many languages.

title: Gabriel Garcia Marquez
body: (1927-2014) Colombian Nobel laureate (1982), magic realism master. One Hundred Years of Solitude (1967, 50M+ copies): Buendia family seven generations in Macondo. Love in the Time of Cholera. Blended fantastical with political. Central figure of Latin American Boom. Garcia Marquez was a journalist before becoming a novelist, and his reportage influenced the precise, unsentimental style of his magical realism.

title: Jorge Luis Borges
body: (1899-1986) Argentine writer of philosophical labyrinths. The Library of Babel, The Garden of Forking Paths, The Aleph, Funes the Memorious. Explored infinity, mirrors, mazes, time. Created imaginary books, blurred fiction and criticism. Influenced magical realism, postmodernism, countless writers. Borges was blind for the last 30 years of his life. His story collections Ficciones and The Aleph are among the most influential of the 20th century.

title: Tolstoy: War and Peace
body: (1869) Epic novel of five aristocratic families during Napoleon's invasion of Russia. 580+ characters: Pierre (spiritual seeker), Andrei (ambitious), Natasha (vital). Combines domestic drama with battle scenes (Borodino) and philosophical essays on history. Challenges Great Man theory of history. Pinnacle of world literature. Tolstoy later rejected his earlier novels, embracing Christian anarchism and pacifism. His philosophical works influenced Gandhi's nonviolent resistance.

title: Proust: In Search of Lost Time
body: (1913-1927) 7-volume modernist novel exploring memory, time, art, society. Involuntary memory triggered by madeleine. Examines French society, Dreyfus Affair, love, jealousy, artistic vocation. Extraordinarily long sentences, psychological insight. Anticipated psychoanalysis. Proust's novel contains over 1.2 million words, 2,000+ characters, and is considered the longest novel ever written by a single author.

title: Classical Greek Drama
body: Tragedy and comedy from choral hymns to Dionysus. Aeschylus (Oresteia: justice evolution). Sophocles (Oedipus Rex: fate, Antigone: conscience vs state). Euripides (Medea: psychological realism). Aristotle's Poetics: catharsis, hamartia, anagnorisis. Aristophanes: comedy (Lysistrata). Menander: New Comedy. Established dramatic forms still defining theater. Greek amphitheaters used sophisticated acoustics.
