The Festival of Dionysus was a major Athenian civic institution featuring competitions between playwrights.

title: Modernist Poetry
body: Broke with traditional forms (early 20th century). Pound: make it new, imagism. Eliot: The Waste Land (1922, fragmentation, postwar despair). Yeats: symbolism, cyclic history. Stevens: imagination vs reality. Frost: New England speech. Williams: no ideas but in things. Auden: technical mastery. Transformed what poetry could be. Free verse, which modernist poets championed, had been used occasionally before but became the dominant mode of poetry in the 20th century.

title: Renaissance Painting
body: (14th-16th centuries) Revolutionized Western art with perspective, chiaroscuro, naturalism, humanism. Early: Masaccio, Botticelli. High Renaissance: Leonardo (Mona Lisa, sfumato), Michelangelo (Sistine Chapel), Raphael (School of Athens). Venetian: Titian. Northern: Van Eyck (oil perfection), Durer, Bruegel. The Renaissance saw the birth of modern banking, with the Medici family developing double-entry bookkeeping, letters of credit, and other financial innovations.

title: Baroque Art
body: (c. 1600-1750) Counter-Reformation dramatic style. Tenebrism, diagonal compositions. Italian: Caravaggio (Calling of St. Matthew), Bernini (Ecstasy of St. Teresa). Flemish/Dutch: Rubens (fleshy dynamism), Rembrandt (Night Watch, psychological depth), Vermeer (Girl with Pearl Earring, interior light). Spanish: Velazquez (Las Meninas). Baroque art was a tool of the Counter-Reformation, using emotional engagement and dramatic realism to inspire Catholic faith.

title: Impressionism
body: (1860s-1880s) Captured fleeting light en plein air. Monet's Impression, Sunrise coined term. Visible brushstrokes, pure colors, everyday subjects. Monet (Haystacks, Water Lilies series), Renoir (Luncheon of the Boating Party), Degas (ballet dancers), Morisot. Manet bridged realism. Eight exhibitions transformed the art market and paved way for modern art. The Impressionists were initially rejected by the official Salon and organized their own exhibitions, a model that influenced later avant-garde movements.

title: Cubism
body: (1907-1914) Picasso and Braque revolutionized representation. Analytic Cubism (1909-1912): fragmented forms, multiple viewpoints, monochrome. Synthetic Cubism (1912-1914): collage, brighter colors. Collapsed foreground/background and showed objects simultaneously. Influenced Futurism, Constructivism, Orphism. Parallels Einstein and Freud. Picasso's Les Demoiselles d'Avignon (1907) anticipated Cubism. The movement took its name from a hostile review by critic Louis Vauxcelles.

title: Modern Art Movements
body: Post-Impressionism: Van Gogh (Starry Night), Cezanne (geometric simplification), Gauguin. Expressionism: Munch (The Scream), Kandinsky. Fauvism: Matisse (color as force). Surrealism: Dali (persistence of memory), Magritte (This is not a pipe). Abstract Expressionism: Pollock (drip), Rothko (color fields). Pop Art: Warhol (Campbell's Soup), Lichtenstein. The Armory Show of 1913 introduced European modern art to America, shocking the public but transforming the American art world.

title: Chinese Painting
body: One of world's oldest continuous traditions (2000+ years). Ink wash painting (shui-mo) on paper/silk. Emphasizes capturing qi (spirit) over realism. Genres: landscape (shan shui), bird-and-flower, figure. Masters: Gu Kaizhi, Wang Wei, Fan Kuan, Ma Yuan, Shen Zhou, Qi Baishi. Inseparable from calligraphy and poetry (three perfections). The four treasures of the Chinese study are brush, ink, paper, and inkstone. Calligraphy is considered the highest form of Chinese painting.

title: Bach and Baroque Music
body: J.S. Bach (1685-1750) pinnacle of Baroque. Major: Mass in B Minor, St. Matthew Passion, Well-Tempered Clavier (48 preludes/fugues), Brandenburg Concertos, Art of Fugue, organ works (Toccata and Fugue in D Minor). Combined German, French, Italian traditions. Obscure until Mendelssohn's 1829 revival. Mathematical precision, emotional depth, spiritual devotion. The Well-Tempered Clavier demonstrated the advantages of equal temperament tuning, enabling composition in all keys without retuning the instrument.

title: Classical Era: Haydn, Mozart
body: (c. 1750-1820) Clarity, balance, form. Haydn (1732-1809): father of symphony and string quartet, 104 symphonies. Mozart (1756-1791): child prodigy, 41 symphonies (Jupiter), 27 piano concertos, operas (Figaro, Don Giovanni, Magic Flute), requiem. Sonata form, minuet, rondo. Vienna musical capital. Piano replaced harpsichord. The symphony orchestra standardized during the Classical era, with four sections: strings, woodwinds, brass, and percussion.

title: Beethoven and Romantic Music
body: Beethoven (1770-1827) bridged Classical and Romantic. 9 symphonies: No.3 Eroica, No.5 (fate), No.6 Pastoral, No.9 Choral (Ode to Joy). 32 piano sonatas (Moonlight, Appassionata), 16 string quartets. Expanded forms and emotional expression. Deafness by 1815, late works composed internally. Heroic struggle defined Romanticism. Roman administration and legal concepts fundamentally shaped European political institutions. Latin influenced all Romance languages.

title: Jazz
body: Originated New Orleans ~1900 from blues, ragtime, marching bands. Early: Louis Armstrong (soloist). Swing: Ellington, Basie, Goodman. Bebop: Charlie Parker (sax), Dizzy Gillespie, Monk. Cool: Miles Davis (Kind of Blue), Coltrane (A Love Supreme). Free: Ornette Coleman. Fusion: Davis (Bitches Brew), Hancock. Influenced rock, hip-hop. Jazz has been called America's classical music and is recognized as a major contribution to world cultural heritage.

title: Rock Music
body: Emerged 1950s America from R&B, country, gospel. Chuck Berry (guitar riffs), Elvis Presley (icon). 1960s: Beatles (Sgt. Pepper's), Stones (blues-based), Dylan (lyrical depth), Hendrix (electric guitar), Pink Floyd (concept albums). 1970s: Led Zeppelin (hard rock), Bowie, Queen, punk. 1980s: Michael Jackson (Thriller), Nirvana (grunge). Dominant global music for 50 years.

title: Electronic Music
body: Uses electronic instruments. Pioneers: musique concrete, Stockhausen, Moog synthesizer. Kraftwerk: electronic pop. House (Chicago, 1980s): Frankie Knuckles, 4/4 beat. Techno (Detroit): Atkins, May, Saunderson. Trance, DnB, dubstep, ambient (Brian Eno). EDM exploded 2010s (Avicii, Daft Punk). Transformed all pop music via synths, samplers, DAWs. Electronic dance music has become a global youth culture phenomenon, with major festivals like Tomorrowland attracting hundreds of thousands of attendees.

title: Ancient Architecture
body: Earliest monuments. Egyptian pyramids (Great Pyramid 2560 BCE, 146m, 2.3M stone blocks). Greek orders: Doric (Parthenon), Ionic, Corinthian. Roman innovations: arches, vaults, concrete. Pantheon (126 CE, 43m dome, largest for 1300 years), Colosseum (50K capacity), aqueducts. Mesoamerican: Mayan pyramids (Chichen Itza), Incan dry-stone (Machu Picchu). Roman concrete, using volcanic ash (pozzolana), set underwater and has survived two millennia. The Pantheon's unreinforced concrete dome remains the world's largest. Ancient builders achieved precision without modern tools.

title: Gothic Architecture
body: (12th-16th centuries) Pointed arches, ribbed vaults, flying buttresses for height and light. Originated at Abbey of Saint-Denis (1140). Cathedrals: Chartres (blue glass, labyrinth), Notre Dame, Reims, Amiens. Rayonnant: Sainte-Chapelle (75% glass). Flamboyant: flame-like curves. Structural daring fused with theological symbolism of light as divine. Gothic builders pioneered the skeletal stone structure, allowing thinner walls and larger windows. The structural system of pointed arches and flying buttresses distributed weight efficiently, enabling cathedrals to reach spectacular heights.

title: Modernist Architecture
body: (Early 20th century) Rejected historical styles for functionalism, new materials, form follows function. Bauhaus (Gropius). Le Corbusier: five points, Villa Savoye, Unite. Mies: less is more, Barcelona Pavilion, Seagram Building. Wright: organic architecture, Fallingwater, Guggenheim. International Style: rectilinear, glass curtain walls. Reshaped cities. The International Style, codified by Henry-Russell Hitchcock and Philip Johnson in 1932, emphasized volume over mass, regularity over symmetry, and elimination of applied ornament. Skyscrapers became symbols of corporate power and urban modernity.

title: Contemporary Architecture
body: Late 20th-21st century diverse approaches. Postmodernism (Venturi, Johnson): historic references. High-tech (Foster, Piano, Rogers): exposed structure, Pompidou Centre, the Shard. Deconstructivism (Gehry, Hadid): fragmented, Guggenheim Bilbao, Heydar Aliyev Center. Sustainable: LEED, passive house. Parametricism: algorithm-driven design. Computer-aided design (CAD) and parametric modeling enable complex organic forms impossible with traditional drafting. Digital fabrication techniques like 3D printing and robotic construction are changing how buildings are designed and assembled.

title: Silent Cinema and Early Film
body: Began 1895 with Lumiere brothers and Melies (Trip to the Moon, 1902). D.W. Griffith advanced film language. German Expressionism (1920s): Cabinet of Dr. Caligari, Nosferatu. Soviet montage: Eisenstein's Battleship Potemkin (Odessa Steps sequence). Chaplin: comedic genius, tramp. The Jazz Singer (1927) introduced sound, ending silent era. Silent films used intertitles for dialogue and employed live musical accompaniment ranging from solo piano to full orchestra.

title: French New Wave
body: (1959-1960s) Handheld cameras, jump cuts, location shooting, autobiographical. Truffaut: The 400 Blows (freeze-frame ending). Godard: Breathless (jump cuts, breaking rules). Rohmer, Rivette, Chabrol, Varda (Cleo from 5 to 7). Cahiers du Cinema critics became directors, advocating auteur theory. Influenced filmmaking worldwide. The French New Wave was made possible by lightweight cameras and synchronized sound equipment that enabled location shooting.

title: Japanese Cinema
body: Kurosawa (Seven Samurai, Rashomon): epic, humanist, influenced Western film (Magnificent Seven, Star Wars). Ozu (Tokyo Story): contemplative, tatami shots, family dynamics. Mizoguchi (Ugetsu): long takes. Miyazaki / Studio Ghibli (Spirited Away): hand-drawn animation masterpieces. Modern: Kore-eda (Shoplifters). J-horror: Ringu. Kurosawa's Rashomon introduced non-linear storytelling to Western cinema and won the Golden Lion at Venice in 1951.

title: Hollywood and American Cinema
body: Studio system (MGM, Warner Bros.) through Golden Age (1930s-1950s). Classical style: continuity editing, closure. Citizen Kane (Welles, 1941): deep focus, non-linear. The Godfather (Coppola, 1972). New Hollywood (1970s): Bonnie and Clyde, Taxi Driver, Apocalypse Now. Blockbusters: Jaws (1975), Star Wars (1977). Scorsese, Tarantino, Nolan. Global dominance. The Hollywood studio system operated like a factory, with stars under contract and all production controlled by studio management until the 1950s.

title: World Cinema Movements
body: Italian Neorealism (1940s): Bicycle Thieves, Rome Open City - real locations, non-professionals, poverty. Indian: Satyajit Ray (Apu Trilogy); Bollywood (masala, song-and-dance). Iranian: Kiarostami, Farhadi (A Separation). Chinese: Zhang Yimou, Chen Kaige. Korean: Bong Joon-ho (Parasite, Palme d'Or 2019). Brazilian: City of God. African: Sembene. World cinema offers diverse storytelling traditions that challenge Hollywood conventions and expand our understanding of cinematic possibilities.

title: United States Overview
body: 9.83 million km2, 334 million. Largest economy ($27T). Federal republic, 50 states. NYC (financial), LA (entertainment), Chicago, Houston, Silicon Valley (tech). Founded 1776. Two-party system (Democrats vs Republicans). Global influence through military (NATO, 800+ bases), dollar reserve currency, technology, culture (Hollywood). Challenges: polarization, healthcare, inequality, gun violence. title: China Overview
body: 9.6 million km2, 1.4 billion. Second-largest economy ($18T). One-party state under Communist Party. Beijing capital; Shanghai largest (26M). Economic miracle since 1978: 800M lifted from poverty. Now high-tech (AI, 5G, EVs, semiconductors). Belt and Road Initiative. Authoritarian governance. Territorial claims South China Sea. Challenges: aging, debt, environment. This knowledge entry provides essential factual information about the subject, including historical context, key developments, and significance in its field.

Culture: anime, manga, cuisine (sushi, ramen). Challenges: median age 48 (oldest), birth rate 1.3, deflation, debt 250% of GDP, energy imports after Fukushima. 100+ active volcanoes. Bollywood, yoga. Challenges: infrastructure, air pollution, water stress, caste system, communal tensions. Geopolitics: border disputes China/Pakistan. Culture: Tolstoy, Dostoevsky, ballet (Bolshoi), Tchaikovsky, first satellite and human in space. Challenges: demographics, sanctions, corruption, Ukraine war (2022 onward). EU leadership. Energiewende: 50%+ renewables. Culture: Bach, Beethoven, Kant, Hegel, Goethe. Challenges: aging, Ukraine war energy transition. Economy: Airbus, LVMH, tourism (80M visitors, most visited). Culture: Revolution, Enlightenment, Impressionism. Challenges: high spending (57% of GDP), pensions, integration. Former British Empire legacy: Commonwealth (56 nations). Brexit (2020). Economy: finance, creative, pharma (AstraZeneca). NHS universal healthcare. Challenges: post-Brexit, Scottish independence debate. title: Brazil Overview

Challenges: climate/bushfires (2019-20 Black Summer), housing, Indigenous reconciliation (Voice referendum failed 2023). ANZUS alliance. body: 1.9 million km2, 278 million. Fourth-most populous. Largest archipelagic state (17,000 islands). New capital Nusantara. Southeast Asia's largest economy ($1.4T). World's largest Muslim population. Exports: palm oil (largest), coal, nickel. Growing digital economy (Gojek). Tourism: Bali, Borobudur. 300+ ethnic groups, 700+ languages. Challenges: deforestation, infrastructure. title: Italy Overview
body: 301,340 km2, 59 million. EU's third-largest economy ($2.2T). Parliamentary republic, Rome. Fashion capital Milan, art Florence, canals Venice. Economy: luxury (Prada, Ferrari, Lamborghini), manufacturing, tourism (Colosseum, Vatican). Cuisine: pasta, pizza, wine. Cultural heritage: Roman Empire, Renaissance (Dante, Leonardo, Michelangelo), opera. Challenges: public debt (140% of GDP), aging, regional inequality (North vs South).

title: Spain Overview
body: 505,990 km2, 47 million. 15th-largest EU economy ($1.6T). Parliamentary monarchy, Madrid. Barcelona (Gaudi architecture), Costa del Sol (tourism). Economy: tourism (2nd most visited), agriculture (olive oil largest), renewable energy. Cultural heritage: Al-Andalus (Islamic period), Flamenco, Don Quixote (Cervantes), Picasso, Dali. Autonomous communities (Catalonia, Basque Country independence movements). Challenges: regional tensions, unemployment.

title: Turkey Overview
body: 783,562 km2, 85 million. Transcontinental (Europe/Asia). Presidential republic, Ankara; Istanbul largest city (16M). Emerging economy ($1.2T). Major: textiles, automotive, tourism, agriculture. NATO member, EU candidate. Cultural heritage: Ottoman Empire, Byzantine (Hagia Sophia), Turkish baths, kebabs. Geopolitical: controls Bosphorus strait. Challenges: inflation, currency crisis, authoritarian drift, earthquake risk (2023 quake 50K+ killed).

title: Saudi Arabia Overview
body: 2.15 million km2, 36 million. Absolute monarchy (King Salman, Crown Prince MBS). World's largest oil exporter (16% of global), third-largest oil producer. OPEC leader. Vision 2030: economic diversification, NEOM megacity, tourism opening. Mecca and Medina (Islam's holiest sites). Conservative Islamic society undergoing social reforms (women driving 2018). Human rights concerns. Regional power competing with Iran. Challenges: oil dependence, water scarcity.

title: Egypt Overview
body: 1 million km2, 110 million. Arab world's most populous. Presidential republic, Cairo (21M metro). Economy ($400B): Suez Canal (12% of global trade), tourism (pyramids), agriculture (Nile delta), gas exports. Ancient civilization: Pharaohs, pyramids, temples. Cultural leader of Arab world (film, music). Muslim Brotherhood political influence. Military as economic power. Challenges: population growth, water stress (Nile dam dispute with Ethiopia), terrorism, economic crisis.

title: Nigeria Overview
body: 923,768 km2, 220 million (Africa's most populous). Federal presidential republic, Abuja; Lagos largest city (24M, Africa's largest). Largest African economy ($500B). Oil (OPEC member, 90% of exports). Tech hub: fintech (Flutterwave, Paystack). Nollywood (second-largest film industry). 250+ ethnic groups (Hausa, Yoruba, Igbo), 500+ languages. Religious split: Muslim north, Christian south. Challenges: oil dependence, corruption, Boko Haram insurgency, infrastructure deficit.

title: South Africa Overview
body: 1.22 million km2, 60 million. Parliamentary republic, Pretoria/Cape Town/Bloemfontein. Most industrialized African economy ($400B). Rich in gold, platinum, diamonds, chromium. Apartheid (1948-1994) ended by Mandela. Rainbow nation: 11 official languages. Johannesburg financial center; Cape Town beauty. Eskom electricity crisis. Challenges: inequality (Gini highest), unemployment (32%), crime, land reform, corruption (state capture).

title: Argentina Overview
body: 2.78 million km2 (8th largest), 46 million. Federal presidential republic, Buenos Aires. Third-largest Latin American economy ($640B). Major: agriculture (soy, beef, wheat), energy (Vaca Muerta shale). Cultural: tango, football (Messi), Maradona, Borges. Peronism dominant political movement. Challenges: chronic inflation (100%+), debt crises (defaulted 9 times), currency controls, poverty (40%). History: Spanish colony, independence 1816, Dirty War (1976-1983).

title: Sweden Overview
body: 450,295 km2, 10 million. Constitutional monarchy, Stockholm. 23rd-largest economy ($600B). Export: Volvo, Scania, Ericsson, IKEA, H&M, Spotify, AstraZeneca. Nordic welfare model: universal healthcare, education, strong social safety net. High taxes, low inequality. Neutral (non-aligned), EU member. Cultural: ABBA, Nobel Prizes, Swedish crime fiction. Challenges: integration of immigrants, housing shortages. This knowledge entry provides essential factual information about the subject, including historical context, key developments, and significance in its field.

title: Netherlands Overview
body: 41,543 km2, 17.5 million. Constitutional monarchy, Amsterdam (capital), The Hague (government). 18th-largest economy ($1T). World's second-largest food exporter (after US) despite small size. Major: Shell, Unilever, Philips, ASML (semiconductor lithography monopoly). Trading hub (Rotterdam port, largest European). 27% below sea level, elaborate dike/ polder system. Liberal social policies. Challenges: rising sea levels, housing crisis.

title: Switzerland Overview
body: 41,285 km2, 8.7 million. Federal republic, Bern. 20th-largest economy ($900B). Per capita GDP highest ($100K+). Banking (UBS, Credit Suisse), pharma (Novartis, Roche), watches (Rolex), chocolates. Four official languages (German, French, Italian, Romansh). Direct democracy (frequent referendums). Neutrality (not EU/UN until 2002). Alps (70% of country). Banks: Swiss banking secrecy eroded by international pressure. Challenges: immigration, EU relations, aging.

title: Singapore Overview
body: 733 km2 (smallest country in SE Asia), 5.5 million. Parliamentary republic, sovereign city-state. 30th-largest economy ($500B). Per capita GDP $80K+ (one of highest). Major hub: shipping (busiest port), finance, tech. Authoritarian democracy (PAP party since 1959). English official language. Multicultural: Chinese, Malay, Indian. Strict laws, low corruption. Founder: Lee Kuan Yew. Challenges: aging, immigration tensions, land constraints, rising costs.

title: Thailand Overview
body: 513,120 km2, 70 million. Constitutional monarchy (King Maha Vajiralongkorn), Bangkok. Second-largest SE Asian economy ($500B). Tourism (40M visitors pre-COVID), agriculture (rice, rubber), manufacturing (autos: Toyota, Honda). Buddhist majority. Cultural: Thai cuisine, temples, Muay Thai, massages. 1932 constitutional monarchy. History: never colonized. Challenges: political instability (coups, 2006, 2014), aging population, inequality.

title: Vietnam Overview
body: 331,212 km2, 100 million. Communist one-party state (15th most populous). Fast-growing economy ($450B). Manufacturing hub (Samsung, Foxconn, Apple). Exports: electronics, textiles, coffee (second-largest), seafood. Communist Party rule since 1975. War: Vietnam War (1955-1975). Doi Moi reforms (1986) opened economy. Cultural: pho, banh mi, Ha Long Bay. Challenges: pollution, infrastructure, corruption, state control.

title: Iran Overview
body: 1.65 million km2, 88 million. Islamic Republic (since 1979 revolution). Tehran capital. Major oil and gas reserves (fourth-largest oil, second-largest gas). Persian culture: Cyrus the Great, poetry (Hafez, Rumi, Ferdowsi). Shia Muslim majority (only country where official). Nuclear program controversy, sanctions. Regional influence through proxies (Hezbollah, Houthis). Mahsa Amini protests (2022, Woman Life Freedom). Challenges: sanctions, economy, political repression.

title: Israel Overview
body: 20,770 km2, 9.5 million. Parliamentary democracy, Jerusalem (contested). Startup Nation: highest R&D spending per capita, 2nd most NASDAQ companies. Major: Intel, Check Point, Waze, Mobileye. Military: Iron Dome, Mossad. Founded 1948 as Jewish state. Conflict with Palestinians (occupation, Gaza wars, settlements). Culture: Hebrew revival, Tel Aviv nightlife, Dead Sea. Challenges: security, Arab/Palestinian conflict, political polarization, Haredi integration.

title: Stoicism
body: Founded by Zeno of Citium (~300 BCE) in Athens. Teaches virtue is the sole good, external events are indifferent. Key discipline: control what you can (judgments, actions), accept what you can't. Three Roman Stoics: Seneca (adviser to Nero, Letters), Epictetus (enslaved-born, Discourses), Marcus Aurelius (emperor, Meditations). Influenced Christian theology and modern CBT. Dichotomy of control remains its most practical teaching.

title: Schopenhauer
body: Arthur Schopenhauer (1788-1860) known for philosophical pessimism. The World as Will and Representation: the noumenal reality is a blind, striving Will that causes suffering. Life oscillates between pain (unfulfilled desire) and boredom (fulfilled desire). Art (especially music) provides temporary escape from Will. Compassion is basis of morality. Influenced Nietzsche, Wagner, Freud, Wittgenstein.

title: Kierkegaard
body: Søren Kierkegaard (1813-1855), father of existentialism. Focused on subjective truth, the leap of faith. Three stages of existence: aesthetic (pleasure), ethical (duty), religious (faith in the absurd). Fear and Trembling: Abraham and Isaac, the teleological suspension of the ethical. Either/Or: aesthetic vs ethical. The Sickness Unto Death: despair as the self's misrelation. Attacked Hegelian system-building and empty Christendom.

title: The Metamorphosis
body: Kafka's The Metamorphosis (1915): traveling salesman Gregor Samsa wakes to find himself transformed into a gigantic insect. The story explores alienation, family dynamics, and identity. Grete (his sister) initially cares for him but ultimately abandons him. The family's financial and emotional dependency on Gregor reverses. Gregor's death brings relief to the family, who go on a picnic. A defining work of 20th century literature.

title: Moby-Dick
body: Herman Melville's Moby-Dick (1851) is an epic novel of Captain Ahab's obsessive quest for the white whale. Through Ishmael's narration, it weaves whaling lore, Shakespearean drama, and philosophical meditation on fate, evil, and the universe. The whale represents different things to different readers: God, nature, fate, the unknowable. The novel was a commercial failure in Melville's lifetime but is now considered a masterpiece of American literature.

title: One Hundred Years of Solitude
body: Gabriel García Márquez's 1967 masterpiece chronicles the Buendía family over seven generations in the fictional Macondo. The novel blends magical realism with Colombian history. Key events: the founding of Macondo by José Arcadio Buendía, the insomnia plague, the banana company massacre, and the final destruction as the prophecies of Melquíades are fulfilled. The novel established magical realism on the world stage and won the Nobel Prize for its author.

title: Catch-22
body: Joseph Heller's Catch-22 (1961) is a satirical war novel set in WWII. The title phrase has entered language meaning a no-win situation. Yossarian, a US bombardier, tries to survive by being declared insane, but Catch-22 says anyone who wants to avoid combat missions must be sane. The novel's circular, non-linear structure mirrors the absurdity of bureaucracy and war. A landmark of postmodern literature capturing the futility and insanity of war.

title: Climate Change Science
body: Earth's average temperature has risen ~1.2C since pre-industrial times due to greenhouse gas emissions (CO2, methane, N2O). IPCC reports represent scientific consensus: human activity is the dominant cause. Consequences: sea-level rise (3.4 mm/year), extreme weather intensification, Arctic sea ice decline, ocean acidification, biodiversity loss. Paris Agreement (2015) aims to limit warming to 1.5-2C. Key emitters: China (28%), US (15%), EU (8%). Carbon budgets constrain future emissions.

title: Renewable Energy
body: Energy from renewable sources: solar photovoltaic (cost dropped 90% in decade), wind (onshore and offshore), hydroelectric, geothermal, biomass, tidal. Renewables now cheapest new electricity in most regions. Global capacity: solar ~1 TW, wind ~900 GW. China leads in deployment. Intermittency challenges: grid storage (lithium batteries, pumped hydro), green hydrogen. Energy transition essential for climate goals. Solar photovoltaics could become the world's largest electricity source by 2030s.

title: Nuclear Energy
body: Nuclear fission produces heat by splitting uranium atoms, generating low-carbon baseload electricity. 440 reactors in 32 countries provide ~10% of global electricity. France leads at 70% of their electricity. Types: PWR, BWR, CANDU. Advanced designs: small modular reactors (SMRs), Generation IV (molten salt, fast reactors). Fusion (ITER project, 35 nations) aims to replicate the Sun's energy but remains decades away. Challenges: waste disposal, safety (Chernobyl 1986, Fukushima 2011), high construction costs.

title: Chaos Theory
body: Chaos theory studies deterministic systems that are extremely sensitive to initial conditions (butterfly effect, Lorenz 1963). Edward Lorenz discovered chaotic behavior in weather models. Key concepts: strange attractors (Lorenz attractor), fractal geometry (Mandelbrot set, 1975), bifurcation diagrams (Feigenbaum constants). Logistic map shows period-doubling route to chaos. Applications: weather prediction limits, turbulence, population biology, economics, and quantum chaos.

title: String Theory
body: String theory proposes fundamental particles are one-dimensional strings whose vibration modes determine particle properties. Requires extra spatial dimensions (10 in superstring theory, 11 in M-theory). Five consistent string theories discovered 1980s, unified by M-theory (Witten 1995). Includes supersymmetry, Calabi-Yau compactifications. Holographic principle: AdS/CFT correspondence (Maldacena 1997) connects string theory to gauge theories. Despite mathematical elegance, no experimental confirmation.

title: Quantum Computing
body: Quantum computing uses quantum bits (qubits) exploiting superposition and entanglement. Types: superconducting (Google, IBM), trapped ions (IonQ), photonic (PsiQuantum). Key algorithms: Shor's (factoring, breaks RSA), Grover's (search, quadratic speedup), quantum error correction (surface codes). Quantum supremacy demonstrated by Google (2019, 53-qubit Sycamore). Challenges: decoherence, error rates, scaling. Current: ~1000 qubits, but fault-tolerant quantum computing likely still years away.

title: Reformation
body: The Protestant Reformation (1517-1648) split Western Christianity. Martin Luther posted 95 Theses (1517) criticizing indulgences and papal authority. Luther's doctrine: sola fide (faith alone), sola scriptura (Bible alone), priesthood of all believers. John Calvin: predestination, Geneva theocracy. Henry VIII: England broke from Rome (1534). Counter-Reformation: Council of Trent (1545-1563), Jesuits (Ignatius Loyola). Religious wars: Thirty Years' War (1618-1648), Peace of Westphalia established modern state sovereignty.

title: Age of Enlightenment
body: The Enlightenment (18th century) emphasized reason, rights, and progress. Key philosophers: Locke (natural rights, government by consent), Montesquieu (separation of powers), Voltaire (free speech, religious tolerance), Rousseau (social contract, general will), Hume (empiricism, skepticism), Kant (dare to know, sapere aude). The French philosophes compiled the Encyclopedie. Adam Smith (Wealth of Nations, 1776) founded modern economics. The Enlightenment inspired the American and French Revolutions and shaped modern democracy, human rights, and secular thought.

title: Cold War: Space Race
body: The Space Race (1955-1975) was Cold War competition between US and USSR for spaceflight supremacy. USSR: Sputnik (first satellite, 1957), Laika (first animal in orbit), Gagarin (first human in space, 1961), Tereshkova (first woman, 1963), first spacewalk (Leonov 1965). US: Apollo program, Kennedy's Moon speech (1961), Apollo 11 (Armstrong and Aldrin first on Moon, July 20, 1969), Nixon called the greatest week since creation. The race drove massive technological advances. Led to space stations (Salyut, Skylab, Mir, ISS) and international cooperation.

title: Film: Citizen Kane
body: Orson Welles's Citizen Kane (1941) is considered the greatest American film. The narrative uses flashbacks and multiple perspectives to investigate the meaning of Rosebud, Kane's dying word. Innovations: deep focus cinematography (Gregg Toland), non-linear narrative, expressionistic lighting, realistic sound. Welles (co-writer, director, star) was 25. The film was controversial for its resemblance to William Randolph Hearst. Despite initial mixed reception, it revolutionized film technique and storytelling.

title: Film: The Godfather
body: Francis Ford Coppola's The Godfather (1972) is a landmark crime film based on Mario Puzo's novel. It chronicles the Corleone family under Vito (Brando) and Michael (Pacino). Themes: American Dream, family, power, corruption. Gordon Willis's cinematography (dark, amber tones), Nino Rota's score. The baptism montage (editing between baptism and murders) is one of cinema's most famous sequences. Won Best Picture, Best Actor (Brando declined). Often ranked second only to Citizen Kane.

title: Architecture: Frank Lloyd Wright
body: Frank Lloyd Wright (1867-1959) was America's greatest architect. Organic architecture: buildings in harmony with nature. Prairie style: Robie House, low horizontal lines, open plans. Fallingwater (1935): cantilevered over Bear Run waterfall, iconic. Guggenheim Museum (NY, 1959): continuous spiral ramp, revolutionary museum form. Taliesin (Wisconsin) and Taliesin West (Arizona) his homes/schools. Imperial Hotel Tokyo (survived 1923 earthquake). Usonian houses: affordable American homes. 1,000+ designs, 500+ built.

title: Biochemistry
body: Studies chemical processes in living organisms. Key biomolecules: proteins (amino acids, enzymes), carbohydrates (sugars, starch), lipids (fats, membranes), nucleic acids (DNA, RNA). Metabolism: catabolism (breaking down) and anabolism (building up). Key cycles: glycolysis, Krebs cycle (TCA), oxidative phosphorylation, photosynthesis. Enzymes catalyze reactions with specificity and regulation. Biochemistry underpins molecular biology, medicine, and biotechnology.

title: Chemical Bonding
body: Atoms bond through electromagnetic interactions. Types: ionic (electron transfer, NaCl), covalent (electron sharing, H2), metallic (electron sea, metals), hydrogen bonds (water), van der Waals forces. Lewis structures represent valence electrons. VSEPR theory predicts molecular geometry. Molecular orbital theory describes bonding through orbital overlap. Bond polarity and electronegativity differences determine bond character. Hybridization (sp3, sp2, sp) explains carbon bonding (tetrahedral, trigonal, linear).

title: Electrochemistry
body: Studies chemical reactions involving electron transfer. Electrochemical cells: galvanic/voltaic (spontaneous, battery) and electrolytic (non-spontaneous, electrolysis). Standard reduction potentials predict cell voltage. Nernst equation relates voltage to concentration. Batteries: lead-acid (car), lithium-ion (phones, EVs, Nobel 2019 to Goodenough, Whittingham, Yoshino). Fuel cells (hydrogen to electricity). Corrosion (rusting) is electrochemical. Electroplating, water electrolysis for hydrogen production.

title: Green Chemistry
body: Designs chemical products and processes minimizing hazardous substances. 12 principles: waste prevention, atom economy, less hazardous synthesis, safer solvents, energy efficiency, renewable feedstocks, catalysis, biodegradability. Paul Anastas coined the term (1998). Examples: supercritical CO2 as solvent, biocatalysis, microwave-assisted synthesis, water-based reactions. Reduces environmental impact while improving efficiency and cost. Growing regulatory and industrial adoption.

title: Natural Disasters Overview
body: Catastrophic events from natural processes. Geological: earthquakes (most deadly are magnitude 7+), volcanic eruptions (Tambara 1815 caused global cooling), tsunamis (2004 Indian Ocean 230K killed). Meteorological: hurricanes/cyclones (Katrina 2005, 1,800 killed), tornadoes (US averages 1,200/year), floods (1931 China floods 2 million+ killed), droughts, heatwaves, wildfires. Impacts worsened by climate change and development in hazard-prone areas. Early warning and building codes reduce casualties.

title: Environmental Issues
body: Anthropogenic environmental degradation. Climate change (1.2C warming, accelerating). Biodiversity loss (1 million species threatened with extinction). Deforestation (10 million hectares/year, Amazon, Congo, SE Asia). Ocean acidification (30% increase since industrial revolution). Plastic pollution (11 million tons annually into oceans, 8 million tons macroplastic). Water scarcity (2 billion lack safe water). Air pollution (7 million premature deaths/year). Ozone depletion (Montreal Protocol 1987 successfully phased out CFCs, hole slowly healing).

title: Biodiversity Hotspots
body: 34 globally recognized biodiversity hotspots covering only 2.3% of Earth's land surface but containing 50% of endemic plant species and 42% of terrestrial vertebrates. Criteria: at least 1,500 endemic vascular plant species and 30% or less original habitat. Examples: Sundaland (Indonesia), Madagascar, Caribbean Islands, Atlantic Forest (Brazil), Cape Floristic Region (South Africa), Mediterranean Basin, Indo-Burma. Hotspots prioritize conservation investment. Most threatened by habitat loss, climate change, invasive species.

title: Evolution by Natural Selection
body: Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace proposed natural selection (1858). Darwin's On the Origin of Species (1859) provided evidence. Mechanism: variation exists in populations, traits are heritable, differential survival/reproduction leads to adaptive evolution. Key evidence: fossil record (transitional forms), homologous structures, vestigial organs, DNA sequence similarity, direct observation (peppered moth, antibiotic resistance, Darwin's finches). Modern synthesis (1930s-40s) integrated genetics with Darwinian evolution.

title: Cell Biology
body: The cell is the basic unit of life. Cell theory: all living things composed of cells, cells from pre-existing cells. Prokaryotes (bacteria, archaea): no nucleus, simple. Eukaryotes (plants, animals, fungi, protists): membrane-bound organelles, nucleus. Key organelles: nucleus (DNA), mitochondria (energy, ATP), endoplasmic reticulum (protein/lipid synthesis), Golgi (modification/packaging), lysosomes (digestion), chloroplasts (photosynthesis in plants). Cell membrane: phospholipid bilayer, selective permeability. Cell division: mitosis (somatic) and meiosis (gametes).

title: Neuroscience
body: Study of the nervous system. The brain has ~86 billion neurons, each with ~7,000 connections. Neuron: dendrites (input), cell body, axon (output), synapses (chemical/electrical communication via neurotransmitters). Major regions: cerebrum (consciousness, language, reasoning), cerebellum (coordination, balance), brainstem (automatic functions). Neurotransmitters: dopamine (reward, movement), serotonin (mood, sleep), glutamate (excitation), GABA (inhibition). Plasticity: brain rewires itself. Neurodegenerative: Alzheimer's (amyloid plaques, tau tangles), Parkinson's (dopamine loss).

title: Fluid Dynamics
body: Studies fluid motion. Navier-Stokes equations (1822) describe fluid flow, are one of seven Millennium Prize Problems. Key concepts: viscosity, turbulence (chaotic flow, Reynolds number), Bernoulli's principle (pressure decreases with speed, explains lift), laminar vs turbulent flow. Applications: aerodynamics (aircraft wings, drag reduction), hydrodynamics (ship design, pipe flow), meteorology (weather prediction), oceanography (currents), cardiovascular (blood flow). Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulates flow numerically.

title: Lasers and Photonics
body: Laser (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation) invented 1960 by Maiman. Key properties: monochromatic, coherent, collimated. Types: gas (HeNe, CO2), solid-state (Nd:YAG), semiconductor (laser diodes), fiber lasers. Applications: optical communication (fiber optics backbone of internet), barcode scanners, CD/DVD/Blu-ray readers, laser cutting/welding, medical (LASIK surgery, skin resurfacing), LiDAR (autonomous driving, mapping), holography, laser cooling (Bose-Einstein condensation). Photonics is the broader science of light manipulation.

title: The Great Gatsby
body: F. Scott Fitzgerald's The Great Gatsby (1925) captures the Jazz Age and American Dream's corruption. Narrator Nick Carraway observes Jay Gatsby's obsessive pursuit of Daisy Buchanan. The green light, Valley of Ashes (eyes of Doctor T.J. Eckleburg), and lavish parties symbolize wealth, class, and disillusionment. Critically panned on release, it became a staple of American literature. Gatsby's tragic end exposes the emptiness of the Roaring Twenties and the unattainability of the past.

title: Cancer Biology
body: Cancer is uncontrolled cell growth caused by accumulated genetic mutations. Hallmarks (Hanahan and Weinberg): sustained proliferation, growth suppression evasion, apoptosis resistance, replicative immortality, angiogenesis induction, invasion/metastasis, immune evasion, metabolic reprogramming. Oncogenes (mutated proto-oncogenes, e.g., RAS, MYC) drive growth; tumor suppressors (p53, RB) are inactivated. Treatments: surgery, chemotherapy (targets dividing cells), radiation, targeted therapy (imatinib for CML), immunotherapy (checkpoint inhibitors: PD-1/PD-L1, CAR-T cells), hormonal therapy (breast/prostate cancer).

title: Human Genetics
body: Study of human genome: 23 chromosome pairs, ~20,000 protein-coding genes, 3 billion base pairs. Human Genome Project (1990-2003) sequenced the entire genome ($3B). Mendelian inheritance: dominant, recessive, X-linked. Genetic disorders: single gene (cystic fibrosis, Huntington's, sickle cell), chromosomal (Down syndrome), complex (diabetes, heart disease). Technologies: PCR (amplify DNA), DNA sequencing (Sanger, next-gen), CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing (Nobel 2020), GWAS (genome-wide association studies). Personalized medicine tailors treatment to genetic profile.

title: Art: Surrealism
body: Surrealism (1920s-1930s) emerged from Dada, exploring the unconscious, dreams, and irrational. Andre Breton's Surrealist Manifesto (1924) defined it as pure psychic automatism. Key painters: Salvador Dali (The Persistence of Memory, melting clocks, paranoiac-critical method), Rene Magritte (The Son of Man, The Treachery of Images - Ceci n'est pas une pipe), Joan Miro (biomorphic abstract forms), Max Ernst (frottage, collage), Yves Tanguy (dream landscapes). Surrealist techniques: automatic drawing, exquisite corpse. Influenced film (Un Chien Andalou by Dali and Bunuel), literature, and advertising.

title: Music: Opera
body: Opera combines singing, orchestral music, theater, and visual arts. Originated in Florence c. 1600 (Monteverdi's Orfeo). Key periods: Baroque (Handel, Vivaldi), Classical (Mozart: Marriage of Figaro, Don Giovanni, Magic Flute), Bel canto (Rossini, Donizetti, Bellini), Romantic (Verdi: La Traviata, Aida; Wagner: Ring Cycle, Tristan und Isolde), Verismo (Puccini: La Boheme, Tosca, Madama Butterfly), Modern (Strauss, Berg). Famous voices: Caruso, Callas, Pavarotti. Major houses: La Scala (Milan), Metropolitan Opera (NY), Royal Opera House (London), Sydney Opera House.

title: Ghana Overview
body: 238,533 km2, 33 million. West African nation, stable democracy. Accra capital. Economy: gold (2nd largest African producer), cocoa (2nd largest), oil (Jubilee field). First sub-Saharan African country to gain independence (1957, Kwame Nkrumah). English official language. Rich culture: kente cloth, Highlife music. Notable: peaceful transitions of power, relatively low corruption. Challenges: debt, cocoa price volatility, illegal gold mining (galamsey).

title: Kenya Overview
body: 580,367 km2, 55 million. East African economic hub. Nairobi capital. Economy: agriculture (tea, coffee, flowers), tourism (safari, Maasai Mara), services, fintech (M-Pesa mobile money pioneer). Swahili and English official. Rift Valley (human origins, Olduvai Gorge). Athletics dominance (long-distance runners). Challenges: corruption, drought cycles, infrastructure, terrorism (Al-Shabaab). This knowledge entry provides essential factual information about the subject, including historical context, key developments, and significance in its field.

title: Behavioral Economics
body: Behavioral economics integrates psychology into economics, challenging the rational actor model. Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky pioneered prospect theory: people value gains and losses asymmetrically (loss aversion), overweight small probabilities, and use mental shortcuts (heuristics) leading to systematic biases. Nudge theory (Thaler and Sunstein) shows how choice architecture influences decisions. Key biases: anchoring, confirmation, availability, framing, status quo, sunk cost. Behavioral economics transformed public policy (organ donation opt-out, retirement savings auto-enrollment). Kahneman Nobel 2002, Thaler Nobel 2017.
